where A = measured anisotropy, A0 = lower baseline, A1 = upper baseline of the
ﬁrst transition, [R]t = total concentration of ﬂuorescent probe, [L]t = total
concentration of protein, KD1 = dissociation constant of the ﬁrst transition, A2 =
upper baseline of the second transition, and KD2 = dissociation constant of the
second transition. If a second transition was not detected, the ﬁt was performed
omitting the second binding term.
Electrophoretic mobility shift assays. To validate select apparent binding afﬁ-
nities, binding reactions were assembled as in the ﬂuorescent polarization experi-
ments described above using trace DNA or RNA radiolabeled with 32P. Samples
were electrophoresed on an 8% native polyacrylamide gel supplemented with 0.5x
TBE buffer (45 mM Tris–HCI, 45 mM borate, pH 8.1, 1 mM EDTA) buffer and ran
at 200 V for 30 min at room temperature. Gels were dried, imaged using a Typhoon
PhosphoImager (Molecular Dynamics), and quantiﬁed by measuring the integrated
signal density of the free and bound bands by the ImageQuant software suite. Data
were ﬁt in Kaleidagraph as a Langmuir binding isotherm. For stoichiometric
EMSAs, the above reactions were assembled with either trace radiolabeled RNA or
DNA supplemented with 2 µM RNA or DNA, respectively.
Heteronuclear single quantum coherence NMR. 15N-HSQC spectra were col-
lected on an Oxford 600 MHz magnet equipped with an Agilent console and HCN
PFG cold probe. Spectra were collected with ~500 µM 15N-labeled Sox2-HMG in
NMR Buffer (10 mM NaH2PO4, pH 7.0, 10 mM DTT, 0.02% NaN3) as free protein
or in fully-saturated 1:1.5 Sox2-HMG:nucleic acid complex with DNA or RNA at
pH 6.5 and 25 °C. Spectra were analyzed via NMRPipe73 and visualized by
CcpNmr Analysis74.
Cell culture. Male mouse embryonic stem cells were maintained on 0.1% gelatin
(EmbryoMax) coated plates in mESC media (KnockOut DMEM, Gibco), 12.6%
fetal bovine serum (EmbryoMax), 1x MEM non-essential amino acids (Gibco), 1x
GlutaMAX supplement (Gibco), Pen-Strep (100 U/mL penicillin and 100 mg/mL
streptomycin, Gibco), 100 µM 2-mercaptoethanol (Gibco), 1000 U/mL LIF
(ESGRO), 1 µM PD0325901 (Sigma-Aldrich), and 3 µM CHIR99021 (Sigma-
Aldrich) to maintain pluripotency. Media was changed daily and cells were pas-
saged every 2–3 days.
Formaldehyde crosslinking. The fRIP protocol was adapted from a previous
study57. Using 15-cm plates, mESC media was aspirated and the cells were washed
once with 25 mL of RT 1x phosphate buffered saline (PBS) followed by submersion in
15 mL of room temperature PBS. Formaldehyde was added to 0.1%, allowed to
crosslink for 10 min at room temperature, and quenched with the addition of glycine
to 125 mM for 5 min. Cells were lifted, collected, and at 4 °C, spun down at 230 × g for
5 min. The pellet was washed twice with 10 mL of ice-cold PBS with centrifuge speeds
of 230 × g 5 min for the ﬁrst wash and 500 × g 5 min for the second wash, and
followed by resuspension into 1.6 mL of ice-cold PBS. The suspension was centrifuged
at 4000 × g for 75 s at 4 °C. The supernatant was removed, and the pellet was
immediately ﬂash frozen in liquid N2 and stored at −70 °C.
UV crosslinking. Using 15-cm plates, mESC media was aspirated, replaced with
13 mL of ice-cold PBS, and subjected to 400 mJ/cm2 of 254 nm UV radiation (UV
Stratalinker 1800). Cells were collected mechanically and at 4 °C, centrifuged at
500 × g for 5 min. The cell pellet was resuspended in 1 mL of ice-cold PBS and
centrifuged at 4000 × g for 75 s at 4 °C. The supernatant was removed, and the
pellet was immediately ﬂash frozen in liquid N2 and stored at −70 °C.
FRIP and UV-RIP. For fRIP and UV-RIP, cell pellets were resuspended in ice-cold
RIPA lysis buffer (50 mM Tris, pH 8, 150 mM KCl, 0.1% SDS, 1% Triton X-100, 5
mM EDTA, 0.5% sodium deoxycholate, 0.5 mM DTT, 2x protease inhibitor cocktail
(ThermoFisher), and 100 U/mL of RNAseOut (Invitrogen)), allowed to sit for 10 min,
and sonicated (Bioruptor UCD-200) in an ice bath with high power setting and 30 s
on/off for 10 min. At 4 °C the lysed cells were centrifuged at 16.1k × g for 10 min and
the supernatant was collected. The number of cells collected after crosslinking for fRIP
and UV-RIP were normalized to ~6.5 million cells. To preclear the supernatant for
both fRIP and UV-RIP, 33 µL of Dynabeads Protein G (Invitrogen) and 550 µL of
binding/washing buffer (25 mM Tris, pH 7.5, 150 mM KCl, 0.5% NP-40, 5 mM
EDTA, 0.5 mM DTT, 1x PIC, and 100 U/mL RNAseOUT) was added and allowed to
rotate for 45 min at 4 °C. From the pre-cleared supernatant, 50 µL was used as the
input and 25 µL for a pre-Sox2 IP western blot with both being frozen at −20 °C. To
the remaining supernatant, 5 µg of Sox2 antibody (Atlas Antibodies; validation shown
in Supplementary Fig. 9) was added and rotated for 2 h at 4 °C. Next, 50 µL of
Dynabeads protein G was added and allowed to rotate for 1 h at 4 °C. At this point,
25 µL of the supernatant was collected and frozen for a post-Sox2 IP western blot at
−20 °C. The beads were washed twice with 1 mL of binding/washing buffer, the
supernatant was removed, and the beads frozen at −20 °C.
RNA puriﬁcation and library preparation. At RT, to the input and beads, 83 µL of
nuclease-free reverse-crosslinking buffer (1 × PBS, pH 7.4, 2% N-lauroyl sarcosine,
10 mM EDTA, 5 mM DTT, and 964 U/mL RNAseOUT) was added and allowed to
mix well. From the resuspended bead sample, 3 µL was removed, frozen at −20 °C,
and stored for a post-Sox2 IP western blot (Supplementary Fig. 9). To input and
bead samples, 20 µL of proteinase K (New England Biolabs) was added. Samples
were then heated in a thermocycler to 42 °C for 1 h and at 53 °C for another hour
with gentle agitation to break apart settled beads. The input and bead samples were
then vortexed with 1 mL of TRIzol Reagent (Ambion) followed by the addition of
200 µL of chloroform and 20 s of vigorous vortex. Samples were centrifuged at
16k × g for 10 min at 4 °C. The aqueous layer was transferred, mixed with 500 µL of
isopropanol and 1 µL of GlycoBlue Coprecipitant (Invitrogen), and allowed to sit
10 min at 4 °C. Samples were centrifuged for 10 min at 12,000 × g at 4 °C, the
supernatant was decanted, and the pellet allowed to air dry for 5 min. To input and
bead samples, 85 µL of nuclease-free H2O, 10 µL of DNAse I reaction buffer, 1 µL
of DNAse I (New England Biolabs) was added, and allowed to sit at room tem-
perature for 15 min. RNA was cleaned up using RNeasy MinElute Cleanup Kit
(Qiagen) following quick start protocol and eluted in RNAse free H2O and stored
at −70 °C. The cDNA library was generated using KAPA RNA Hyperprep kit with
RiboErase (HMR, Kapa Biosystems), 8 bp xGen dual index UMI adaptors (Inte-
grated DNA Technologies), and sequenced on an Illumina NextSeq 500 using
paired end sequencing of 34 bp.
Computational pipeline for fRIP- and UV-RIP-seq. Sequencing reads were
mapped by STAR75 to mm38 using a gene annotation from GENCODE (vM16)76.
The reads that are not uniquely mapped were discarded. The unique molecular
identiﬁers (UMIs) associated with sequencing reads were counted for gene bodies
in the annotation by using featureCounts77. The enriched genes were identiﬁed by
comparing pull-down with inputs. The fold changes of pull-down relative to input
and their statistical signiﬁcance were calculated by DESeq278 for the genes that
have three or more UMIs in at least one sample. Speciﬁcally, the total number of
UMIs of uniquely mapped reads were used as a normalization factor, and biological
replicate information were considered as a covariate.
Reporting summary. Further information on research design is available in
the Nature Research Reporting Summary linked to this article.
Code availability
Computation code available upon request.
Data availability
The data that support this study are available upon reasonable request. All sequencing data
generated have been deposited in the NCBI GEO database (https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/
gds/) under accession code GSE142013. The source data for Figs. 1, 2c, 3, 4, 6, Supplementary
Figs. 4, 5, 8, and 9, and Supplementary Tables 1 and 7 are provided as a source data ﬁle.

Abstract
Astrocytes play many important functions in response to spinal cord injury (SCI) 
in an activated manner, including clearance of necrotic tissue, formation of pro-
tective barrier, maintenance of microenvironment balance, interaction with im-
mune cells, and formation of the glial scar. More and more studies have shown 
that the astrocytes are heterogeneous, such as inflammatory astrocyte 1 (A1) and 
neuroprotective astrocyte 2 (A2) types. However, the subtypes of astrocyte result-
ing from SCI have not been clearly defined. In this study, using single-­cell RNA 
sequencing, we constructed the transcriptomic profile of astrocytes from unin-
jured spinal cord tissue and injured tissue nearby the lesion epicenter at 0.5, 1, 
3, 7, 14, 60, and 90 days after mouse hemisection spinal cord surgery. Our analy-
sis uncovered six transcriptionally distinct astrocyte states, including Atp1b2+, 

2 of 21  |     
HOU et al.
1  |  INTRODUCTION
Spinal cord injury (SCI) is a serious neurological disorder 
that can cause physical dependency, neurological dys-
function, permanent disability, and serial complications. 
Globally, there are 250 000 to 500 000 new cases per year, 
with the number of cases per million people rising from 
236 to 1298 over the past 30 years.1 However, SCI is incur-
able because the nerve tissue is difficult to repair and re-
generate.2 Pathologically, SCI consists of two phases: the 
primary time after SCI that involves the mechanical injury 
(primary injuries), and the second time after SCI that in-
volves disruption of vascular, oxidative stress, production 
of free radical, infiltration of immune cells, and growth of 
glial scar (secondary injuries).3,4 The second time after SCI 
can be divided into immediate (first 2 h), acute (2–­48 h), 
subacute (2–­14 days), intermediate (14–­180 days), and 
chronic (>180 days) phase.3 Secondary injuries are often 
equally or more devastating than primary injuries.4
Astrocytes are the predominant and ubiquitous glial 
cells in the central nervous system (CNS), and play a crit-
ical role in the blood–­brain barrier, metabolism, synaptic 
modification, and maintenance of neurons.5After SCI, as-
trocytes are involved in almost all injury stress responses 
and wound healing processes through activation, includ-
ing inflammation, ischemia, infection, and formation of 
a glial scar.6 Reactive astrocyte (RA), which are the main 
component of glial scar (also named astrocytic scar6) 
around the lesion core, have traditionally been considered 
to be the main cause of preventing axonal regeneration 
and functional recovery in both rodents and humans with 
SCI.7,8 However, the spinal cord of some non-­mammalian 
vertebrate animals has been shown to regenerate after 
injury in which glial scar formation is rarely found.6,9 
Therefore, targeting astrocytes to regulate astrocytic scar 
formation is one of the potential therapeutic strategies to 
promote axon regeneration in mammals. Unfortunately, 
double knockout of glial fibrillary acidic protein (Gfap) 
and vimentin (Vim), conditional knockout of Stat3 by mG-
FAPCre and selective ablation of scar-­forming astrocytes to 
inhibit the formation of glial scar failed to promote axon 
regeneration but resulted in extensive injury and lim-
ited functional recovery in SCI mouse model.10–­12 These 
results were due to the neuroprotective and neurotoxic 
roles of astrocytes following SCI.13,14 Identifying the het-
erogeneity of astrocytes can contribute to understand the 
role of astrocytes in SCI. However, the heterogeneity and 
functional differences of astrocytes in SCI have not been 
clearly defined.
Neuroinflammation in chronic neurodegenerative 
disorders (such as Alzheimer's disease and Parkinson's 
disease) and acute injury (such as ischemia) induced two 
different types of reactive astrocyte, termed “A1” and “A2,” 
respectively. Neuroinflammation-­induced A1 astrocytes 
upregulated many genes which are destructive to syn-
apses, suggesting that A1 astrocytes might have “harmful” 
functions.15 By contrast, ischemia-­induced A2 astrocytes 
upregulated many neurotrophic factors which promote 
survival and growth of neurons, suggesting that A2 astro-
cytes might have “helpful” functions.15 Modern develop-
ments in single-­cell RNA sequencing techniques are likely 
to provide a wealth of information about the heterogene-
ity of astrocytes at the cellular level. Recent studies have 
identified six subtypes of astrocytes in wildtype and trans-
genic model of AD (5xFAD) mice16 and eight types in the 
brain of AD patients.17 Similar to AD, the process of SCI is 
accompanied not only by inflammation, but also by isch-
emia, hemorrhage, and growth of glial scar.18 Therefore, 
the transcriptomic profile of astrocytes at high resolution 
can help analyze astrocyte's cellular heterogeneity in SCI.
To analyze and identify the heterogeneity and dynamic 
evolution of astrocytes after SCI, we performed single-­cell 
of Neuroregeneration, Nantong 
University, Nantong 226001, People's 
Republic of China.
Email: nervegu@ntu.edu.cn
Funding information
NSFC | Key Programme, Grant/Award 
Number: 81730067; NSFC | Major 
Research Plan, Grant/Award Number: 
81991514
S100a4+, Gpr84+, C3+/G0s2+, GFAP+/Tm4sf1+, and Gss+/Cryab+ astrocytes. We 
used these new signatures combined with canonical astrocyte markers to deter-
mine the distribution of morphologically and physiologically distinct astrocyte 
population at injured sites by immunofluorescence staining. Then we identified 
the dynamic evolution process of each astrocyte subtype following SCI. Finally, 
we also revealed the evolution of highly expressed genes in these astrocyte sub-
types at different phases of SCI. Together, we provided six astrocyte subtypes at 
single-­cell resolution following SCI. These data not only contribute to understand 
the heterogeneity of astrocytes during SCI but also help to find new astrocyte sub-
types as a target for SCI repair.
K E Y W O R D S
astrocytes, heterogeneity, single-­cell RNA sequencing, spinal cord injury

      |  3 of 21
HOU et al.
RNA sequencing to characterize the transcriptional pro-
file of SCI tissues in the acute phase (0.5d, 1d), subacute 
phase (3d, 7d, 14d), intermediate phase (60d, 90d) and un-
injured tissue. We identified six molecularly distinct astro-
cyte subtypes, including Atp1b2+, S100a4+, Gpr84+, C3+/
G0s2+, GFAP+/Tm4sf1+, and Gss+/Cryab+ astrocytes. And 
these astrocyte populations were confirmed by immunos-
taining in SCI and uninjured spinal cord tissue. We found 
that astrocytes were less heterogenous in the uninjured 
spinal cord, but more diverse in SCI tissue. The dynamic 
cellular evolution of astrocytes was revealed by cell per-
centage during the SCI process. Gene Ontology analysis 
based on up-­regulated genes in each astrocyte population 
revealed that these astrocytes had different functions for 
SCI pathological development. Identification of the het-
erogeneity and functional differences of astrocytes in our 
study will lead to a better understanding of astrocytes 
during the process of SCI and help to find a new astrocyte 
population as a target to promote spinal cord tissue repair.
2  |  MATERIALS AND METHODS
2.1  |  Animal
Both male and female C57Bl/6 mice at two-­month-­old 
were used for single-­cell sequencing. B6.129P2(Cg)-­
Cx3cr1tm2.1(cre/ERT2)Litt/WganJ19 
(stock 
021160) 
and 
Olig1Cre20,21; Rosa26-­Loxp-­Stop-­Loxp-­tdTomato (Olig1Cre; 
LSL-­tdT)22,23 mice were generous gifts from Dr. Guoqiang 
Wang and Dr. Guiquan Chen, respectively. The mice were 
maintained in the core animal facility of the Model Animal 
Research Center at Nanjing University. The temperature 
of the animal room was 25 ± 1°C and the relative humidity 
was 55%. The light cycle of the animal room was automati-
cally controlled (12 h light and dark cycle). Mice had free 
access to food and water. Mouse breeding was conducted 
under an animal protocol approved by the Institutional 
Animal Care and Use Committee (IACUC). All the experi-
ments were performed under the Guide for the Care and 
Use of Laboratory Animals of Nanjing University.
2.2  |  Spinal cord injury and post-­
surgical care
Two-­month-­old C57Bl/6 mice were anesthetized with 
100 mg/kg ketamine and 10 mg/kg xylazine, lateral hemi-
section leading to monoplegia as previously described.24 
Briefly, the thoracic 10 (T10) of the spinal cord was ex-
posed, we carefully used a micro knife and under a mi-
croscope to over-­hemisection only at the right side of 
the spinal cord midline. Sugi tips were used to stop the 
bleeding as well as possible. Secure the skin with clips. 
The spinal cord injury (SCI) mouse was put in the wake-
­up cage at 35°C until awaking. The study included scRNA-­
seq profiles from uninjured and seven-­time points groups: 
post-­SCI 0.5 days (pSCI-­0.5d) (4 female and 3 male mice), 
pSCI-­1d (3 female and 3 male mice), pSCI-­3d (3 female 
and 3 male mice), pSCI-­7d (3 female and 3 male mice), 
pSCI-­14d (3 female and 3 male mice), pSCI-­60d (2 female 
and 2 male mice) pSCI-­90d (3 female and 3 male mice) 
and uninjured (3 female and 3 male mice). For pSCI-­3d, 
pSCI-­7d, pSCI-­14d, pSCI-­60d, and pSCI-­90d group mice, 
we manually emptied bladders twice daily until sacrifice 
or recovery of full sphincter control. All SCI mice were 
checked for body weight, clinical signs of pain, or infec-
tion. We euthanized mice that were too painful, infected, 
or had more than a 15% decrease in body weight.
2.3  |  Single-­cell isolation and scRNA-­seq 
library preparation
SCI mice are anesthetized by intraperitoneal injection of 
ketamine (100 mg/kg) and xylazine (10  mg/kg) and put 
into the ice immediately for 5 min. Subsequent of the proto-
cols were kept on the ice except for digestion. 1-­cm segment 
centered on the lesion site from SCI mice and an equiva-
lent segment from uninjured mice were performed to iso-
late single cells. Spinal cord tissue was moved membrane 
and transferred into ice-­cold RNase-­free DPBS (14 190 144, 
Thermo Fisher) with 2% FBS (SV30087.02, Thermo Fisher), 
washed three times by DPBS to remove residual free cells 
and blood. Carefully dissected and minced tissue was di-
gested in 0.25% (W/V) trypsin (25200-­072, Thermo Fisher) 
with 10 mg/ml DNase I (11284932001, Sigma) for 10 min at 
37°C. Termination of digestion by adding FBS to 5% (V/V), 
the cell suspension was gently triturated through pipettes 
ten times and filtered by a 40 μm sieve to remove large pieces 
of undigested tissue. The filtrate was centrifuged for 20 min 
at 300g to remove the debris and myelin sheath. The cell 
pellet was gently re-­suspended in appropriate DPBS (+2% 
FBS) into 700 cells/μl. Cells were stained with 0.4% Trypan 
blue (Thermo Fisher, Cat. No.14190144) to check the vi-
ability of Countess II Automated Cell Counter (Thermo 
Fisher). The quality of single-­cell suspension is controlled 
by the ratio of live cells versus death cells (>80%) and single 
cells versus cell clumps (>95%).
The suspension of 10 000–­20 000 single cells was loaded 
into the Chromium single cells gene expression platform 
(10× Genomics). Single-­cell libraries were prepared and 
connected by Beads with unique molecular identifier 
(UMI) and cell barcodes were loaded close to saturation, 
to ensure that each cell was paired with a Bead in a Gel 
Beads-­in emulsion. After cell lysis, polyadenylated mRNA 

4 of 21  |     
HOU et al.
molecules hybridized into the Beads. Followed by bar-
coded reverse transcription of RNA (cDNA), cDNA ampli-
fication, enzymatic fragmentation, 5′ adaptor attachment, 
and tissue indexing. All the remaining procedures were 
performed according to the standard manufacturer's pro-
tocol. Approximately 5180–­15 144 single cells were re-
covered and the libraries were purified, quantified, and 
then sequenced on NovaSeq6000 (Illumina) using 2 × 150 
chemistry (average depth of ~30 000–­50 000 reads).
2.4  |  Alignment and preprocessing of 
droplet-­based scRNA-­seq data
ScRNA-­sequence tissues were processed, normalized, 
and filtered by using the Cell Ranger 2.1.0 pipeline with 
default and recommended parameters. FASTQs gen-
erated from Illumina sequencing output were aligned 
to the GRCm38.p6 (mm10, NCBI: GCA_000001635.8) 
mouse reference genome, using the STAR algorithm.25 
For each tissue, counting UMIs and filtering non-­cell as-
sociated barcodes were used to generate a Gene-­Barcode 
digital matrix of gene expression. The quality control and 
downstream analysis of our single-­cell transcriptome data 
using the Seurat (v3.0.2) R toolkit.26 In each tissue, based 
on median genes per cell, we calculated and removed 
the outliers cells using a boxplot. stats to remove double 
cells.27 For each cell, we excluded cells that were detected 
fewer than 400. For each gene, we excluded a gene that 
was detected in fewer than 10 cells. The mitochondria 
genes were calculated using Percentage Feature Set func-
tion of the Seurat package.26 And the cells with more than 
20% expression of mitochondria genes were excluded to 
remove low activity cells. These single-­cell transcriptome 
data were normalized using Normalize Data function in 
the Seurat package. In normalized data, we extracted a 
subset of variable genes which were identified while con-
trolling for the strong relationship between variability and 
average expression. Finally, we integrated data from dif-
ferent tissues after identifying anchors between datasets 
using the Find Integration Anchors and Integrate Data in 
the Seurat package.23–­25
2.5  |  Single-­cell data analysis
After scaled single-­cell data, we performed principal com-
ponent analysis (PCA) and reduced the data to the top 
30 PCA components. T-­distributed stochastic neighbor 
embedding (t-­SNE) was used to visualize the clusters on 
a 2D map.28 After computing a shared nearest neighbor 
graph,26 identification of cell types and subtypes by non-
linear dimensional reduction (t-­SNE) cells were clustered 
using graph-­based clustering of the PCA reduced data 
with the Louvain Method.29 We applied the same proce-
dure of scaled, dimensionality reduction, and clustering 
to the specific set of data for sub-­clustering. We used the 
Wilcoxon Rank-­Sum Test to find significant deferentially 
expressed genes by comparing the remaining clusters for 
each cluster. SingleR and known marker genes were used 
to identify cell types.30
2.6  |  Subclustering of astrocytes
To analyze and identify the population of astrocytes and 
reactive astrocytes, we screened for astrocytes by the 
expression of marker genes, as described previously.16 
Briefly, in the single-­cell database mentioned above, the 
cells we selected meet two conditions: (1) cells express one 
of Gfap, Gss, Aldh1l1, Slc1a3, AldoC, Aqp4, Id3, Fabp7, 
and Lcn2; (2) cell did not express Dock2 and Cx3cr1 in the 
same one cell to exclude macrophages and microglia. For 
these selected cells, the same analyses described in the 
“Single-­cell data analysis” section were performed.
2.7  |  Enrichment analysis
Up-­regulated and down-­regulated expression genes (Log-­
fold change > 0.7 and adjusted p values < .05) from each 
cluster were subjected to Metascape for Gene Ontology 
(GO) and Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes 
(KEGG) analysis.31 The top 14 GO terms and KEGG path-
ways were reported.
2.8  |  Cell proportion
For the calculation of cell proportion, we set the total 
number of cells screened in each sample as Nt. For exam-
ple, the total number of cells in pSCI-­0.5d was Nt = 10 318 
cells. And we set the number of each Cluster in each sam-
ple as Nc. For example, the number of Cluster 4 cells in 
pSCI-­0.5d (not the total number of Cluster 4 cells) was 
Nc = 684 cells, So the cell proportion of Cluster 4 at pSCI-­
0.5d was 6.63 (Nc/Nt × 100). In this way, the cell pro-
portion of each Cluster in each sample is calculated and 
connected with a smooth line.
2.9  |  Heatmaps
For cell percentage heatmap (Figure 1E), the percent of 
cells per cluster in each tissue was calculated and plotted 
using the Pheatmap package in R. For heatmaps of gene 

      |  5 of 21
HOU et al.
expression of Clusters 1 or 4, which have a high percent-
age in the uninjured tissue, firstly, the genes expressed 
in Clusters 1 or 4 in uninjured tissues were used as refer-
ence genes to calculate the up-­regulated top 15 genes in 
Clusters 1 or 4 cells at each time point, and these genes 
were combined into a gene set, the gene in this set was 
shown in each cell of Clusters 1 or 4 by heatmap. For 
Clusters 3 or 2, 5, and 9 cells, which were rare or absent in 
FIGURE 1  Single-­cell transcriptional profiling of spinal cord injury and uninjured tissue. (A) Schematic of single-­cell isolation and

HOU et al.
uninjured tissue, we used the total of uninjured tissue ex-
pressed genes as the reference gene to perform the above 
calculation.
2.10  |  tSNE plots of gene expression
tSNE plots of gene expression were calculated using log2 
Max count in each cell. The picture was exported via the 
Gene/Feature Expression function in Loupe Browser 
(4.2.0). For tSNE plots double gene co-­expression in the 
same cell, we used the Filters function in Loupe Browser, 
and the condition was set that the count >0 of each gene.
2.11  |  Immunofluorescence staining
pSCI tissue was fixed for more than 4 h in 4% paraform-
aldehyde and transferred into 30% (W/V) sucrose in PBS 
overnight. The tissue was embedded in O.C.T compound 
and sectioned sagittally at the thickness of 10  μm in a 
Microtome Cryostat. These slices were kept at −20°C 
until used. The slices were dried at RT for 10  min and 
55°C for 10 min. After washing by PBS and blocking by 
10% serum in PBS with 0.2% Triton X-­100, the slices were 
exposed to primary antibodies, including rabbit anti-­Aqp4 
(1:200, Alomone, AQP-­004), mouse anti-­Id3 (1:100, Santa 
Cruz, sc-­56712), goat anti-­Fabp7 (1:500, R&D, AF3166), 
mouse anti-­AldoC (1:500, Santa Cruz, sc-­271593), rab-
bit anti-­Iba1 (1:500, Wako, 019–­19741), mouse anti-­
GFAP (1:250, Santa Cruz, sc-­6534), rabbit anti-­GFAP 
(1:1000, Abcam, ab7260), mouse anti-­S100a4 (1:500, NSJ 
Bioreagents, V8068), mouse anti-­G0S2 (1:200, Santa Cruz, 
sc-­518067), goat anti-­C3(1:50, R&D, AF3677-­SP), rabbit 
anti-­Tm4sf1(1:500, LSBio, LS-­B7077), goat anti-­Mki67 
(1:400, Santa Cruz, sc-­7846), mouse anti-­Olig2 (1:500, 
Millipore, MABN50) and incubated overnight at 4°C. And 
then secondary Alexa-­flour conjugated antibodies (1:500, 
Jackson Immuno) were added to the slices for 1 h at RT. 
DAPI (1:10 000, Sigma-­Aldrich, D9542) was used to stain 
the nucleus. Imaged were taken on a Zeiss LSM880 confo-
cal microscope.
3  |  RESULTS
3.1  |  Single-­cell transcriptional profiling 
of spinal cord injury and uninjured tissue
To obtain single-­cell transcriptome profiling for spi-
nal cord injury (SCI) site, we performed droplet-­based 
single-­cell RNA sequence (scRNA-­seq, 10× Genomics 
Chromium) using SCI tissue from lesions site at acute 
(post-­SCI 0.5 days and 1 day, named as pSCI-­0.5d and 
pSCI-­1d, respectively), subacute (pSCI-­3d, pSCI-­7d, and 
pSCI-­14d), intermediate (pSCI-­60d and pSCI-­90d) phase 
(Figure 1A), as well as uninjured thoracic 10 (T10) spinal 
cord tissue (Detail in Methods). To obtain an unbiased un-
derstanding of the transcriptome of all types of astrocytes 
associated with SCI, scRNA-­seq was performed from cells 
isolated from the dissociated tissue without any preselec-
tion. More than 4 mice for each group and a total of 47 
mice were used to collect 103 420 sequenced. Following 
the removal of poor-­quality cells and double cells accord-
ing to the method in Guo et al. study,27 we obtained high-­
quality transcriptional data for 86 972 cells that we then 
subjected to unsupervised clustering by an independent 
component analysis (ICA)-­based approach and visualizing 
by t-­distributed stochastic neighbor embedding (tSNE).32 
Based on the expression of well-­known cell-­type-­specific 
markers, these clusters were categorized into microglia, 
macrophages, granulocytes, endothelial cells, epithelial 
cells, erythrocytes, monocytes, astrocytes, oligodendro-
cytes, B cells, and T cells (Figure S1A). Their relative fre-
quency ranged from 0.20% to 11.45% (Figure 1B,C). The 
failure of adult spinal cord neurons to be captured was at-
tributed to their susceptibility to death during enzymatic 
dissociation.33 Among these cells, there were 5 microglia 
clusters and 6 macrophage clusters, and they were phase-­
dependent(Figure 1B–­D). For example, the microglia of 
Cluster 6 (C6) mainly existed in uninjured tissues and 
gradually disappeared in SCI tissue (Figures 1D,E, S1B–­I); 
however, in SCI tissues, microglia of the C1, C5, C9, and 
C15 replaced C6 cells (Figure S1B–­I). In addition, the het-
erogeneity of macrophages was similar to that of microglia, 
and the evolution of different clusters of macrophages was 
closely related to the time after SCI (Figures 1D,E, S1B–­I). 
The reason that macrophages were detected in uninjured 
tissues was that tissue perfusion was not performed to 
clear the blood during the separation process to minimize 
in vitro transcriptional activation, and the macrophage 
present in spinal cord blood was included in the sequenc-
ing (Figure S1B).
Studies have shown that the accumulation of granu-
locytes in the spinal cord lesion is associated with pro-­
inflammatory cytokines, apoptosis, and oxidative stress,34 
we found that the granulocyte cells of C3 quickly re-
sponded to injury and accumulated at the site of the 
wound during the acute time after SCI (Figure  S1C,D), 
but it was cleared during the subacute time after SCI 
(Figure S1E–­G). However, in the intermediate time after 
SCI, the granulocyte cells of C3 were re-­recruited into 
the site of the wound, which is associated with chronic 
inflammation of SCI (Figure S1H,I).35 Together, these re-
sults suggested that our scRNA-­seq data can accurately 
reflect the evolution of cell biology following SCI.

      |  7 of 21
HOU et al.
3.2  |  Clustering and 
annotation of astrocytes
To reveal the heterogeneity of astrocytes and their dy-
namic evolution following the SCI process, we focused 
on astrocytes. However, we found that only C10 cells 
were clustered and annotated as astrocytes by Seurat 
and SingleR26 (Figure 1B), which was mainly present in 
the uninjured tissue and gradually decreased after SCI 
(Figures 1D, S1B–­I). There are many reactive astrocytes 
(RAs) around the wound during the SCI process,2,7 but we 
did not find RAs population in these clusters (Figure 1B). 
This is because RAs show intrinsic heterogeneity in gene 
expression compared with naïve astrocytes,14 but the vis-
ual clustering of tSNE in individual cells is based on simi-
larity in gene expression.26,35 Since some RAs during SCI 
exhibited non-­astrocytic properties, they were clustered 
into other cell populations (Figure S2). For example, cells 
expressing glial fibrillary acidic protein (Gfap), a canoni-
cal marker of RAs, were present in almost every cluster 
(Figure S2A). For cluster analysis of astrocytes, 33 692 
cells that expressed canonical astrocyte marker (count 
≥ 1), including Gfap, aldolase, fructose-­bisphosphate C 
(AldoC), aquaporin 4 (Aqp4), fatty acid-­binding protein 
7 (Fabp7), solute carrier family 1 member 3 (Slc1a3), al-
dehyde dehydrogenase 1 family member L1 (Aldh1l1), 
glutathione synthetase (Gss), inhibitor of DNA binding 
3 (Id3), and lipocalin 2 (Lcn2)36,37 (Details in Methods) 
(Figure S2) were selected as astrocyte-­like cells for fur-
ther analysis and identification. These cells were rescaled 
and re-­clustered into 12 clusters (Figure  2A). Cluster 
sizes ranged from 0.07% to as many as 56% of all cells in 
FIGURE 2  Clustering and annotation of astrocytes. (A) tSNE visualization of the re-­clustered astrocyte population showing 12 
distinguishable clusters. (B) Relative frequency of astrocyte clusters in each experimental group. Cluster 1 (blue) starts at the top of the 
graph, with increasing numbers going from top to bottom. (C) tSNE visualization showing a single astrocyte plot of each group. (D–­K) tSNE 
plots of expression pattern of astrocyte-­associative gene, including Gfap (D), Vim (E), Lcn2 (F), CD44 (G), Stat3 (H), Rela (I), Nfia (J), and C3 
(K). Values shown are log-­normalized counts per cell.

8 of 21  |     
HOU et al.
uninjured or each time point of SCI (Figure 2B). The per-
centage of these clusters in each group was fluctuant fol-
lowing the SCI (Figures 2B,C, S3A–­H). And these clusters 
were also phase-­dependent (Figures 2B,C, S3A–­H). After 
7 days of SCI, glial scar began to form, and Cluster 1 cells 
accounted for the most, followed by Cluster 3 cells, in-
dicating that Clusters 1 and 3 astrocytes were the main 
constituent cells in the early stage of glial scar forma-
tion (Figure 2B,C). After that, Clusters 1 and 3 gradually 
decreased, and Clusters 2, 5, and 9 gradually increased, 
which together with Clusters 1 and 3 formed a mature 
glial scar (Figure 2B,C).
The expression of RAs-­related markers or transcrip-
tion factors was ordered and interlaced in these clusters, 
for example, Gfap was highly expressed in Clusters 4 and 
6 (Figure  2D); Vimentin (Vim) was highly expressed in 
Clusters 3 and 6 (Figure 2E); Lcn2 was highly expressed in 
Cluster 2, 5, and 9 (Figure 2F); CD44, which is a marker 
of pan-­reactive astrocyte,38 was highly expressed in Clusters 
2, 3, 5, 7, and 9 cells (Figure 2G). The expression of signal 
transducer and activator of transcription 3 (Stat3) and RELA 
proto-­oncogene, NF-­kB subunit (Rela) which was the key 
transcription factor for RAs from naïve astrocytes were rel-
atively consistent across clusters (Figure  2H,I). However, 
the expression of nuclear factor I A (Nfia), a transcription 
factor that regulates astrocyte development and reactive as-
trogenesis,39 and complement C3 (C3), a marker of neuroin-
flammatory activated astrocytes,40 were almost completely 
non-­overlapping, which represent astrocytes into activating 
and activated state, respectively (Figure 2J,K).
At pSCI-­0.5d, when astrocytes were activated by injury, 
we found that the relative percentages of Gfap+, Lcn2+, 
Vim+, CD44+, C3+, and Rela+ cells in the total cells in-
creased simultaneously (Figure S4A–­D,F,H,I), but peaked 
at different time points. For example, the percentage of 
Gfap+ cells reached a peak at pSCI-­1d, slightly fluctu-
ated from pSCI-­1d to pSCI-­14d, and gradually decreased 
after SCI-­14d (Figure S4A,I). The proportion of Vim+ cells 
reached a peak at pSCI-­3d and remained high from pS-
CI-­7d to pSCI-­90d (Figure S4B,I). The percentage of Lcn2+ 
cells peaked at pSCI-­0.5d and pSCI-­90d but remained low 
in the subacute phase (Figure S4C,I).
3.3  |  Atp1a2-­expressing astrocytes 
became A2 astrocytes in the acute and 
subacute phases and A1 astrocytes in the 
intermediate phase
To determine the evolution of naïve astrocytes after SCI, 
Cluster 4 cells were first analyzed because of their high per-
centage of cells in uninjured tissue (Figure S3A). We chose 
three canonical astrocyte genes, Aqp4, Fabp7, and Id3, to 
examine their expression pattern in this Cluster. The rea-
son was as follows. Recent studies have demonstrated that 
Aqp4 is specifically expressed in the end-­feet of astrocytes 
in the central nervous system,36 and that Fabp7 and Id3 are 
abundantly expressed in astrocytes in the adult brain.41,42 
Gene expression analysis showed that Aqp4, Fabp7, and 
Id3 were highly expressed in Cluster 4 cells (Figure 3A,B). 
Cluster 4 cells decreased sharply at the acute time after 
SCI (pSCI-­0.5d), and almost disappeared during the suba-
cute phase (pSCI-­14d). Interestingly, a few Cluster 4 cells 
reappeared in the injured tissue at the intermediate phase 
(pSCI-­60d and 90d) (Figure 3C), and these Cluster 4 cells 
did not express Gfap (Figure S4A). Gene expression analy-
sis showed that the ATPase, Na+/K+ transporting, beta 2 
polypeptide (Atp1b2), a marker of astrocyte, was signifi-
cantly enriched in Cluster 4 cells (Figure 3D–­E), suggesting 
that Atp1b2 may be used to label Cluster 4 astrocyte.
To detect the distribution of Aqp4, Fabp7, and Id3-­
expressing astrocytes, we performed immunofluorescence 
staining on Aqp4, Id3, and Fabp7 using uninjured, pSCI-­1d, 
and pSCI-­90d tissue sections (Figure 3F). In uninjured tis-
sue, Aqp4, Id3, and Fabp7 were co-­expressed in astrocytes 
(Figure 3F) which were consistent with single-­cell sequenc-
ing (Figure S5A–­C). At the acute time after SCI (pSCI-­1d), 
Aqp4, Id3, and Fabp7 were activated and their expression 
increased in Cluster 4 cells (Figure  3F,G) but decreased 
after pSCI-­1d due to the reduction of Cluster 4 cells (Figure 
S5A–­C). However, at the intermediate time after SCI (pSCI-­
90d), we found that Id3+ cells were located in the core of 
the wound and Fabp7+ cells were located in the boundary 
(Figure 3F,G). These results indicated that Cluster 4 astro-
cytes evolved into different types of RAs after injury.
To reveal the change of relatively highly-­expressed genes 
in the remnant Cluster 4 cells following the SCI process, we 
analyzed and listed the top 15 up-­regulated genes in Cluster 
4 cells from pSCI-­0.5d to pSCI-­90d compared with unin-
jured Cluster 4 cells (Figure S5D). We found that the Cluster 
4 cells highly expressed chemokine (C–­C motif) ligands 
family (Ccls), C–­X–­C motif chemokines (Cxcls), and S100a 
family at pSCI-­0.5d (Figure S5D). The important function 
of Ccls and Cxcls is to recruit monocytes to impact neuro-
logical remission,43 and S100 protein is an EF-­hand Ca2+ 
protein family involved in intracellular calcium homeosta-
sis.44 These results suggested that Cluster 4 astrocytes were 
involved in recruiting monocytes in response to injury and 
tended to maintain the calcium homeostasis in the lesion. 
These genes expression indicated that Cluster 4 cells in the 
acute and subacute time after SCI were helpful and tend to 
be A2 astrocytes. However, the multiple neuroinflamma-
tory cytokine cathelicidin antimicrobial peptide (Camp), 
C1qa, C1qc, C1qb, eukaryotic translation elongation factor 
1 alpha 1 (Eef1a1) were highly expressed in Cluster 4 cells 
at pSCI-­90d (Figure S5D), which tends to be A1 astrocytes.

      |  9 of 21
HOU et al.
3.4  |  Cluster 1 cells 
had the characteristics of both 
astrocyte and microglia after SCI and 
highly expressed Gpr84
Intermediate state astrocytes, which share molecular 
characteristics of both microglia and astrocytes, has 
been found in the injury and chronic neurodegeneration 
brains of mouse and human.45 However, it is not known 
whether this type of astrocyte is also present in SCI tis-
sue. Since AldoC is primarily expressed in astrocytes,46,47 
it was used as an additional marker for molecular anal-
ysis. We found that AldoC, Slc1a3, Gss (Figure  4A,B), 
and Gfap (Figure 2D) were expressed by Cluster 1 cells. 
Unexpectedly, 86.9% of Cluster 1 cells expressed C-­X3-­C 
motif chemokine receptor 1 (Cx3cr1) (Figure S6B,C), a 
marker of microglia. In 11 036 Cluster 1 cells, 36.4% cells 
co-­expressed Slc1a3 and Cx3cr1, 14.6% cells co-­expressed 
FIGURE 3  Atp1a2-­expressing astrocytes became A2 astrocytes in the acute and subacute phase and A1 astrocytes in the intermediate 
phase. (A) tSNE plot of Cluster 4 cells. (B) tSNE plots of the expression pattern of Aqp4, Fabp7, and Id3. (C) Proportion of Cluster 4 cells 
among all re-­clustered astrocytes in each group. (D) Violin plot showing the expression level distributions of Atp1b2 in each cluster. (E) 
tSNE plot of the expression pattern of Atp1b2. (F) Co-­immunostaining of Aqp4, Id3, and Fabp7 on uninjured, pSCI-­1d, and pSCI-­90d tissue. 
The scale bar is 100 μm. (G) Enlargements of the white boxes in F. The scale bar is 25 μm.

10 of 21  |     
HOU et al.
AldoC and Cx3cr1, and 16.7% cells co-­expressed Gfap and 
Cx3cr1 (Figure S6D). These results indicated that Cluster 
1 cells were intermediate state cells with dual charac-
teristics of astrocytes and microglia. Similar to Cluster 4 
cells, the proportion of Cluster 1 decreased sharply during 
the acute time after SCI (Figure 4C). The difference was 
that the percentage of Cluster 1 cells increased from less 
than 20% to 60% during the subacute time after SCI while 
remaining >30% during the intermediate time after SCI 
(Figure 4C). Moreover, we found that G protein-­coupled 
receptor 84 (Gpr84) was highly expressed in Cluster 1 
cells (Figure 4D,E), especially in SCI tissue (Figure S6G). 
Therefore, Gpr84 may be considered an important molec-
ular feature for Cluster 1 cells.
To verify the presence of this subtype in SCI tis-
sue, we performed immunofluorescence staining of 
AldoC and Iba1 or GFAP and Iba1 on the spinal cord of 
Cx3cr1tm2.1(Cre/ERT2)Litt (Cx3cr1Cre) mice which expressing 
EYFP driven by Cx3cr1-­promoter.19 Similar to recent stud-
ies,48 almost all EYFP+ cells were co-­labeled by Iba1, and a 
few EYFP+ cells expressed weak AldoC or Gfap in the un-
injured spinal cord (Figure 4F–­I). However, we found that 
high expression of Gfap or AldoC in EYFP+/Iba1+ cells 
in or around the injured core at pSCI-­7d and pSCI-­14d 
(Figure 4F–­I). These results suggested that Cluster 1 cells 
were present in SCI tissue and have the characteristics of 
both microglia and astrocyte.
To reveal the process of gene expression changes in 
Cluster 1 cells after SCI, we performed up-­regulated ex-
pression analysis of Cluster 1 cells at each time point, 
and Cluster 1 cells of uninjured tissue were used as ref-
erence (Figure S6F). We found that most of the genes 
up-­regulated in Cluster 1 after SCI were similar to those 
up-­regulated in Cluster 4, such as Ccl family, S100a 
family, secreted phosphoprotein 1 (Spp1), Lyz2, Vim, and 
Lgals1/3 (Figure S6F). In addition, Cluster 1 also ex-
pressed many up-­regulated genes different from Cluster 
4 in response to damage. For example, Il1rn, which is a 
key immune controller,49 is highly expressed in Cluster 
1 cells of pSCI-­0.5d, and to avoid immune hyperactiva-
tion and triggering immune infection in normal spinal 
cord tissues. A disintegrin and metallopeptidase domain 
8 (Adam8), which is activated by TNFα and highly ex-
pressed in RAs,50 was found to be highly expressed in 
Cluster 1 cells of pSCI-­0.5d, indicating that Cluster 1 
cells of pSCI-­0.5d responds to TNFα signaling through 
Adam8 (Figure S6F). Fibronectin (Fn1), an endogenous 
repair protein that contributes to wound healing,51 was 
significantly up-­regulated in Cluster 1 cells of pSCI-­1d 
and pSCI-­3d (Figure S6F). Glycoprotein nonmetastatic 
melanoma protein B (Gpnmb), a neuroprotective pro-
tein in a cerebral ischemia–­reperfusion injury model,52 
was up-­regulated in Cluster 1 of pSCI-­3d, pSCI-­7d, 
and pSCI-­60d (Figure S6F). Fatty acid-­binding protein 
5 (Fabp5) is a cytoplasmic protein responsible for the 
binding, transportation, and signal transduction of 
Fatty acids and lipophilic molecules.53 Studies have 
shown that Fabp5 is necessary for neurite growth and 
cell survival, and is expressed at low levels in adult 
brain tissue, but at high levels in damaged central ner-
vous system tissue.53 Indeed, we found that the expres-
sion of Fabp5 significantly increased in Cluster 1 cells 
from pSCI-­1d to pSCI-­90d (Figure S6F). Apolipoprotein 
C1 and E (Apoc1 and Apoe) are important lipid and cho-
lesterol metabolic proteins and have a neuroprotective 
role in neurological disease and injury.54,55 We found 
that the expression of Apoe and Apoc1 was significantly 
increased in Cluster 1 cells from pSCI-­3d to pSCI-­90d 
(Figure S6F). These results suggested that Cluster 1 was 
associated with clearance of necrotic tissue and com-
posed of the glial scar.
These differentially expressed genes could be enriched 
to significantly activated GO terms, including neutrophil 
chemotaxis, inflammatory response, fibrosis, IL-­17 sig-
naling pathway, neuronal death, cell killing, and so on 
(Figure S6E). These results suggested that Cluster 1 was 
involved in the immune response after injury, the death 
of damaged nerve cells, and the fibrosis of the glial scar 
(Figure S6E). In addition, we found that the genes regulat-
ing endocytosis were mainly expressed in Cluster 1 cells 
(Figure S6E), indicating that Cluster 1 cells were involved 
in the clearance of necrotic tissue. In contrast, the GO 
terms enriched by downregulated genes include regula-
tion of glial cell migration, brain development, gliogene-
sis, cell activation, and so on (Figure S6E), indicating that 
downregulated genes were related to the function of naïve 
astrocytes.
3.5  |  Cluster 3 cells may be an 
activated state of Cluster 4 cells with 
neuroprotective effects
Cluster 3 cells were almost absent in uninjured tissue (2.2% 
of total cells), increased sharply in the acute time after SCI 
and peaked at pSCI-­3d (35% of total cells) (Figure 5A,B). 
However, the cluster 3 cells gradually decreased after 
pSCI-­7d and were maintained at 5–­7% in the interme-
diate of SCI (Figure 5A,B). We found that the Cluster 3 
cells not only expressed astrocyte marker gene Fabp7, 
Gfap, Gss, AldoC (Figures 2D, 3B, 4B, and S7A) but also 
expressed RAs marker genes including Vim, C3, and Lcn2 
(Figures 2E,F,K, and S7A). Differential expression analy-
sis showed that Cluster 3 cells highly expressed S100a4 
(also known as Mts1) (Figure 5C,D), which is highly ex-
pressed in stress-­activated astrocytes in the injured central

nervous system and has neuroprotective functions.56 
Indeed, in the immunofluorescence staining of Gfap and 
S100a4, we found that S100a4 was expressed in Gfap+ cells 
at pSCI-­0.5d and pSCI-­1d, but rarely expressed at pSCI-­
90d (Figure 5E,F).
To reveal the enrichment function of Cluster 3 cells 
in response to SCI, we performed GO analysis, and the 
sequence profile from all cells of uninjured tissue as ref-
erence genes, because there were too few Clusters 3 cells 
in uninjured tissue. The top 14 upregulated pathways in 
Cluster 3 cells, including SRP-­dependent cotranslational 
protein targeting to membrane, neutrophil degranula-
tion, salmonella infection, parvulin-­associated pre-­rRNP 
complex, platelet activation, signaling and aggregation, 
response to interferon-­gamma, and so on (Figure S7B). 
Like Cluster 1 cells, Cluster 3 cells were also involved in 
the inflammatory response, but the difference was that 
Cluster 3 cells regulate neutrophils by degranulation, 
while Cluster 1 cells regulate neutrophil chemotaxis 
(Figure S7B). Consistent with Cluster 1, GO enriched 
with down-­regulated genes in Cluster 3 cells also was 
associated with the function of naïve astrocytes (Figure 
S7B). Differential expression analysis showed the top 15 
upregulated genes in Cluster 3 cells at each time point 
of SCI (Figure S7C). We found that Vim and Spp1 main-
tained highly expressed levels from pSCI-­0.5d to pSCI-­
14d (Figure S7C). At pSCI-­0.5d and pSCI-­1d, Cxcl3, 
Cxcl2, Arg1, Ccl2, Thbs1, and heme oxygenase 1 (Hmox1) 
were activated and expressed in Cluster 3 cells, but after 
pSCI-­1d, Gpnmb, Lgals1/3, and Lyz2 were expressed 
(Figure S7C). Since Anderson and colleagues previously 
used a bulk-­RNA sequencing technique to demonstrate 
that Cxcl3, Cxcl2, Arg1, Ccl2, Thbs1, Hmox1, Gpnmb, 
Lgals1/3, and Lyz2 are expressed significantly higher in 
astrocytes in the spinal cord with injury than in those 
without injury.10 To compare gene expression profiles by

the bulk-­RNA sequencing and the scRNA sequencing, 
we analyzed the above genes. In line with the Anderson 
et al study,10 we found that the expression of these genes 
was increased, suggesting that these molecules may be 
involved in the regeneration of the spinal cord after 
injury. In addition, S100a10 and epithelial membrane 
protein 1 (Emp1), which are specific genes of neuropro-
tective A2 reactive astrocyte,38,57 were found to be highly 
expressed in Cluster 3 cells (Figure 5G). Together, these 
results indicated that Cluster 3 cells were neuroprotec-
tive A2 astrocytes in SCI.
3.6  |  Clusters 2, 5, and 9 cells were Gfap-­
low astrocytes and highly expressed G0s2
Clusters 2, 5, and 9 cells showed high expression of Lcn2, 
Vim, and C3, but little expressed Gfap (Figure 2D–­F,K). 
Similar to Cluster 3 cells, the percentage of Clusters 2, 
5, and 9 cells was lower in the uninjured tissue (Figure 
S3A). However, the percentage of Cluster 2 cells was 
sharply increased in the acute time after SCI and peaked 
at pSCI-­1d, while it maintained low in the subacute time 
after SCI (Figure 6A,B). But in the intermediate time after 
FIGURE 6  Clusters 2, 5, and 9 cells were Gfap-­low astrocytes and highly expressed G0s2. (A) tSNE plot of Clusters 2, 5, and 9 cells. 
(B) Graph represents the proportion of Clusters 2, 5, and 9 cells among all re-­clustered astrocytes in each group. (C) tSNE plot of the expression 
pattern of G0S2. (D) Violin plot showing the expression level distributions of G0S2 in each cluster. (E) Co-­immunostaining of G0S2, C3, and Gfap 
on uninjured, pSCI-­1d, pSCI-­7d, and pSCI-­90d tissue. The scale bar is 100 μm. (F) Enlargements of the white boxes in E. The scale bar is 25 μm.

14 of 21  |     
HOU et al.
SCI, the percentage of Clusters 2, 5, and 9 cells was in-
creased (Figure 6A,B). Differential gene expression analy-
sis showed that G0/G1 switch gene 2 (G0s2), a cell cycle 
regulator that commits a cell to enter the G1 phase from 
G0, was specifically expressed in Clusters 2, 5, and 9 cells 
(Figure 5C,D). G0s2 was co-­expressed with C3, Vim, and 
Lcn2, but not Gfap (Figure S8A). G0s2 and C3 could be 
used as subtype-­specific markers of Clusters 2, 5, and 9 
cells (Figure S8A). To confirm these results, we performed 
immunofluorescence staining on G0s2, C3 and Gfap, find-
ing that some of the C3+ cells could be labeled by G0s2 
but not Gfap at pSCI-­d, pSCI-­7d, and pSCI-­90d, indicat-
ing that Clusters 2, 5, and 9 cells existed around the injury 
core (Figure 6E,F). G0s2 was highly expressed at pSCI-­0.5d 
and pSCI-­1d, decreased from pSCI-­3d to pSCI-­14d, and in-
creased at pSCI-­60d and pSCI-­90d (Figure S8B), which was 
consistent with the percentage of Clusters 2, 5, and 9 cells.
We also performed the GO analysis of the up-­regulated 
genes in Clusters 2, 5, and 9 cells of SCI tissue compared 
with the uninjured tissue, and identified the top upregulated 
pathways in Clusters 2, 5, and 9 cells, including neutrophil 
degranulation, modification of morphology or physiology 
of other organism, RHO GTPase effectors, inflammatory 
response, and so on (Figure S8C). Activation of the RHO 
GTPase effectors and RHO GTPase activate NADPH oxidases 
signaling pathway in CNS injury promotes cell death and loss 
of neural processes and synapses.58 These results suggested 
that Clusters 2, 5, and 9 cells primarily respond to oxida-
tive stress and promote cell death in injured tissue. Synapse 
pruning, response to metal ions, sodium ion export across the 
plasma membrane, and other functions are downregulated 
in Clusters 2, 5, and 9 cells (Figure S8C). To reveal the ex-
pression characteristics of Clusters 2, 5, and 9 cells following 
the SCI, we showed the top 15 upregulated genes in Clusters 
2, 5, and 9 cells following SCI compared to uninjured tissue 
sequencing (Figure S8D). We found that the genes highly ex-
pressed in Cluster 2 were consistent with those in Clusters 
5 and 9 cells after the acute time after SCI (Figure S8D), in-
cluding Ngp, Camp, S100a8, S100a9, Lcn2, Lrg1, and so on. 
Therefore, Clusters 2, 5, and 9 cells were grouped into one 
subpopulation of astrocytes. We further analyzed the highly 
expressed genes between Clusters 2, 5, and 9 cells. Compared 
with the other two types of cells, Cluster 2 cells highly ex-
pressed Il1b, Gm5483, Cxcl2, Clec4d, Cxcl3; Cluster 5 cells 
highly expressed Ifitm6, Ly6g, Ltf, Plbd1, Adpgk; Cluster 9 
cells highly expressed Elane, Hist1h1b, Ctsg, Top2a, Ube2c.
3.7  |  Small populations of proliferative 
astrocyte emerge in SCI tissue
Cluster 6 cells were found in uninjured and SCI tissue, 
highly expressing Gfap and Vim (Figure S4A,B). After 
SCI, the percentage of Cluster 6 cells gradually increased, 
peaked at pSCI-­3d, and gradually decreased and main-
tained at 5% after pSCI-­7d (Figure  7A,B). Differential 
gene expression analysis revealed that transmembrane 
4  L six family member 1 (Tm4sf1) which is considered 
to be a marker gene of type A2 RAs59 was specifically 
expressed in Cluster 6 cells (Figure  7C,D). We found 
that Sox2, Sox9 (Figure  7E), and Nfia (Figure  2J) were 
highly expressed in Cluster 6 cells. In addition, Hes1, a 
key transcription factor in the Notch signaling pathway, 
Smad1/5, a key transcription factor in the Jak/Stat3 sign-
aling pathway, and midkine (Mdk), solute carrier family 
6 member 8 (Slc6a8), which play an important regulatory 
role in astrocyte development, were expressed in Cluster 
6 cells (Figure 7E). Tm4sf1 was co-­expressed with Sox2, 
Sox9, Nfia, Stat3, Slc1a3, Vim, and Gfap in Cluster 6 cells 
(Figure S9A). Tm4sf1 and Gfap could be used as subtype-­
specific markers of Cluster 6 cells (Figure S9A). Zbtb20, a 
recently identified marker of astrocyte precursor cells,60 
was also expressed in Cluster 6 cells (Figure 7E). At the 
transcriptional level, we found that the Cluster 6 cells 
also expressed proliferating cell markers proliferating cell 
nuclear antigen (Pcna), minichromosome maintenance 
complex component 2 (Mcm2), and marker of proliferation 
Ki-­67 (Mki67) (Figure S9B). Thus, we defined the Cluster 
6 astrocytes as proliferating RAs. To verify the existence 
of Cluster 6 cells, we also performed immunofluorescence 
staining of Tm4sf1 and Gfap and found that some of the 
Gfap+ cells were indeed labeled by Tm4sf1 at pSCI-­0.5d, 
and pSCI-­7d (Figure  7F,G). In addition, Tm4sf1+ cells 
could be co-­labeled with Mki67 (Figure 7H). These results 
suggested that Cluster 6 cells were proliferating RAs.
GO analysis identified the top up-­regulated pathways 
in Cluster 6 cells, including glial cell proliferation, micro-
tubule cytoskeleton organization, mitotic cell cycle pro-
cess, and positive regulation of mitotic cell cycle, which 
are related to cell division and proliferation (Figure S9C). 
The top down-­regulated pathways in Cluster 6 cells in-
cluded synapse pruning, positive regulation of response to 
external stimulus, astrocyte development, and neutrophil 
degranulation (Figure S9C). Taken together, these results 
suggested the presence of proliferative astrocytes in SCI 
tissue, and that Tm4sf1 in combination with GFAP can 
specifically label these cells.
3.8  |  Identification of OPCs-­derived 
astrocytes in SCI tissue
Recent evidence has uncovered the presence of astrocytes 
derived from oligodendrocyte precursor cells (OPCs) in 
SCI tissue.58,59 We found that Cluster 7 and 11 cells may be 
OPCs-­derived astrocytes. The percentage of Cluster 7 and

HOU et al.
11 cells in the uninjured tissue was less than 2%, increased 
after SCI, and peaked at pSCI-­1d, fluctuated slightly in the 
subacute time after SCI, and increased again in the inter-
mediate time after SCI (Figure 8A,B). Differential gene ex-
pression analysis revealed that Cluster 7 and 11 cells highly 
expressed crystallin alpha B (Cryab) (Figure 8C,D), which 
has been shown to inhibit neuroinflammation and protect 
cells from apoptosis by inhibiting caspase activity.61 Thus, 
Cluster 7 and 11 cells were considered to be neuroprotec-
tive type A2 astrocytes. Cluster 7 and 11 cells expressed 
multiple RA marker genes, including Gfap (Figure 2D), 
Vim (Figure 2E), Lcn2 (Figure 2F), Stat3 (Figure 2H), and 
naïve astrocyte marker genes, including Nfia (Figure 2J), 
AldoC, Gss (Figure 4B), and Zbtb20 (Figure 7E). We found 
that oligodendrocyte development-­related transcription 
factors Olig1, Olig2, Sox10, and NK6 homeobox 2 (Nkx6.2) 
were expressed in Cluster 7 and 11 cells (Figure 8E), so 
we defined that Cluster 7 and 11 cells were OPCs-­derived 
astrocytes.
In addition, we generated OPCs lineage-­tracing 
(Olig1Cre;LSL-­tdT) mice by Olig1Cre mice crossing with 
StopLoxP/LoxP-­tdTomato (LSL-­tdT) mice22 and performed 
spinal cord hemisection surgery. We found that in un-
injured tissue, tdT+ and Olig2+ cells were uniformly 
distributed in the spinal cord, while Gfap+ cells were 
mainly distributed in the gray matter (Figure  8F,G). In 
contrast, at pSCI-­7d, both the tdT+ cells and Gfap+ cells 
clustered around the lesion core both in gray matter and 
white matter, and some tdT+ cells strongly expressed Gfap 
(Figure  8F,G). These results indicated that SCI stimu-
lated OPCs to accumulate around the wound, and caused 
some OPCs to express the marker genes of astrocytes. The 
marker genes expressed in Clusters 7 and 11 cells were 
similar to those expressed in Clusters 4 and 6 cells, such 
as Cryab and Gfap or Zbtb20 and AldoC were co-­expressed 
in Clusters 4, 6, 7, and 11 cells (Figure S10A), indicating 
that OPCs-­derived astrocytes were similar to naïve astro-
cytes and proliferative astrocytes. Cryab and Gss could be 
used as subtype-­specific markers of Clusters 7 and 11 cells 
(Figure S10A).
GO analysis identified the top upregulated pathways 
in Clusters 7 and 11, including ensheathment of neurons, 
oligodendrocyte differentiation, axon development, glial 
cell development, protein localization to axon, response 
to the metal ion, and supramolecular fiber organization 
(Figure S10B).
4  |  DISCUSSION
Our results distinguish six astrocyte subpopulations at 
single-­cell transcriptomic level in SCI tissue, and these 
six subpopulations could be identified by a single or dual 
marker. Based on the upregulated genes, we also analyzed 
the different functions of these six astrocyte subpopula-
tions. To verify the practicability of this RA classification 
method, we validated six astrocyte subpopulations by im-
munofluorescence staining, using the antibody of a new 
marker of astrocyte subpopulations and the canonical 
marker of astrocyte. And our results demonstrated that 
this method could be used to discover astrocyte subpopu-
lations in SCI tissue. In addition, the analysis of SCI tissues 
at seven-­time points could accurately reflect the evolution 
of six astrocyte subpopulations. Based on the expression of 
marker genes of A1 and A2 astrocytes in the six subpopu-
lations of astrocytes, we can also distinguish whether they 
are “helpful” or “harmful” astrocytes. Importantly, by this 
study, we were able to gain several novel insights into as-
trocyte subpopulations. The identification of changes in 
single-­cell transcription, which control the conversion of 
cells from an uninjured state to an SCI state in different 
astrocyte populations, can help identify specific molecular 
processes and mechanisms for finding new therapeutic in-
tervention targets.
Although the transcription profile of astrocytes had 
been previously studied in genome-­wide RNA sequenc-
ing of astrocyte-­specific ribosome-­associated RNA 
(ramRNA) precipitated via a haemagglutinin tag bulk 
RNA-­seq studies at pSCI-­14d,10 our scRNA-­seq data 
and analysis of seven-­time points of SCI gave us direct 
insight into the astrocyte subtypes in which specific 
genes were expressed. For example, gap junction protein 
(Gja1), which is significantly decreased in the pSCI-­14d 
tissue,10 was found to be highly expressed in Cluster 4 
(Atp1a2+) cells of uninjured tissue, while not expressed 
at pSCI-­14d (data not shown). Similarly, cell growth and 
differentiation protein Lrg1, which is significantly in-
creased in the pSCI-­14d tissue,10 was found to be highly 
expressed in Clusters 2, 5, and 9 cells from pSCI-­0.5d 
to pSCI-­90d, but not expressed in uninjured tissue (data 
not shown). The cluster-­specific gene expression pat-
terns during SCI shown in this study help to elucidate 
regulatory mechanisms and can provide information 
for targeted functional studies to regulate these genes in 
specific RA subtypes.
Recently, the single-­cell transcriptome database of 
mouse SCI sites at pSCI-­1d, pSCI-­3d, and pSCI-­7d has 
been reported.33 We found the same pattern in their clus-
ter analysis as in our cluster analysis, that the number of 
clustered astrocytes did not reflect the true number of as-
trocytes in the injury site. However, they clustered only 
930 astrocytes in a total of 66 178 cells,33 and we cluster 
only 2918 astrocytes in a total of 86 972 cells (Figure 1B,C). 
Notably, neither their astrocyte cluster nor our astrocyte 
cluster expressed RAs specific genes. It is an inevitable 
fact that leukocytes, endothelial cells, and ependymal 

      |  17 of 21
HOU et al.
cells are more likely than neural cell types to survive our 
dissociation procedure than neural cells, however, the het-
erogeneity of astrocytes in injured tissues should be an-
other important factor. Therefore, it is difficult to cluster 
the astrocyte subtypes without astrocyte selection. Our 
new clustering approach highlights the heterogeneity of 
astrocytes that is masked in the current default calculation 
methods.
FIGURE 8  Identification of OPCs-­derived astrocytes in SCI tissue. (A) tSNE plot of Cluster 7 and 11 cells. (B) Proportion of Cluster 
7 and 11 cells among all re-­clustered astrocytes in each group. (C) tSNE plot of the expression pattern of Cryab. (D) Violin plot showing 
the expression level distributions of Cryab in each cluster. (E) tSNE plots of the expression pattern of Olig2, Olig1, Sox10, and Nkx6.2. (F) 
Co-­immunostaining of Gfap and Olig2 on uninjured and pSCI-­7d tissue of Olig1Cre;LSL-­tdT mice. White arrows indicate Olig2+/tdT+ cells 
and cyan arrows indicate Gfap+/tdT+ cells. The scale bar is 100 μm. (G) Enlargements of the white boxes in F. The scale bar is 25 μm. (H) 
Summary of six types of astrocytes.

18 of 21  |     
HOU et al.
Since the Cluster 8 cells were lymphocytes selected by 
Lcn2, and the number of Cluster 10 and 12 cells was too 
small, we did not analyze and identify them.
Considering that the expression of astrocyte marker 
genes may be non-­specific, we compared our data of each 
cluster with previous results. First, Cluster 4 was defined 
as naïve astrocytes because of changes in cell percentage 
as well as gene expression between uninjured tissue and 
SCI tissue. However, with the disappearance of Cluster 
4 cells, the Cluster 3 cells appeared when the damage 
occurred. We found that the highly expressed genes in 
Cluster 3 cells were highly consistent with those in Cluster 
4 cells, Such as Thbs1, S100a10, Ccl2, and Ccl6. Therefore, 
we define Cluster 3 as the active state of Cluster 4. Second, 
Cluster 1 (Gpr84+) cells were intermediate cells with char-
acteristics of both microglia and astrocytes. The existence 
of such cells has been controversial, although single-­cell 
seq data and immunofluorescence staining have con-
firmed their presence in the central nervous system of 
patients with neurodegenerative disease.18,43 Cluster 1 
cells can be clustered from Single-­cell RNA-­seq data be-
cause of the non-­specificity of marker genes at the tran-
scriptional level. However, in Cx3cr1-­derived EYFP mice 
(Cx3cr1CreER), we found that EYFP+ cells with intact cy-
toplasm were labeled as microglia by Iba1, while some 
EYFP+/Iba1+ cells were Gfap+ or AldoC+ cells in SCI 
tissue (Figure 4F,G). In uninjured tissue, although EYFP 
cells labeled with AldoC or GFAP could be found, the ex-
pression of AldoC and Gfap was relatively weak. These 
results are consistent with single-­cell transcriptome data 
(Figures S4A, S6A). Together, our single-­cell sequencing 
and immunofluorescence staining results support the per-
spective that there are intermediate cells with characteris-
tics of both astrocytes and microglia in mouse SCI tissue.45 
Third, Gfap has been traditionally considered to be a pan-­
marker of reactive astrocyte,62 but a growing number of 
studies, especially single-­cell sequencing results, indi-
cated the presence of RAs that did not express Gfap.62,63 
Bulk-­RNA sequencing and differential gene analysis of 
cells isolated from injured and uninjured Aldh1l1-­EGFP 
mice also showed that GFAP expression increased more 
than threefold at 1 week of injury, but at 2 weeks of in-
jury, the GFAP transcription level had no difference from 
that of uninjured mice.64 In addition, in the brain of the 
5xFAD mouse model, Gfap-­high and Gfap-­low reactive 
astrocytes were identified by single-­cell sequencing.16 
Consistent with this result, we also identified a unique 
astrocyte subtype, Clusters 2, 5, and 9, which was Gfap-­
low and highly expression Lcn2, Vim, and C3. Fourth, pro-
liferating astrocytes have also been reported to be a RA 
population in SCI tissue10,65 and we found that Cluster 6 
cells have the potential for proliferating because they ex-
press Sox9, Nifa, and Zbtb20, as well as proliferating cell 
protein Pcna, Mcm2 and Mki67. Fifth, OPCs can produce 
astrocytes during the early stages of central nervous sys-
tem development22 and genetic fate mapping studies have 
demonstrated that a small fraction of OPCs can produce 
astrocytes in SCI tissue.66 Consistent with these studies, 
we found that Clusters 7 and 11 cells not only expressed 
the astrocyte marker gene AldoC, Slc1a3, Gss, Gfap, Vim, 
and Lcn2, but also expressed the OPC marker gene Olig2, 
Olig1, Sox10, and Nkx6.2. Therefore, Clusters 7 and 11 
were defined as OPC-­derived astrocytes.
Astrocytes are currently classified as “harmful” A1 and 
“neuroprotective” A2, which are activated by chronic neu-
roinflammation and acute injury, respectively. Currently, 
specific genes expressed by A1 or A2 have been reported. 
For example, A1 specifically expresses Rela, Ifnγ, C1q 
FIGURE 9  Schematic diagram for changes on astrocyte subtypes after SCI. There are dynamic changes in Clusters 1, 2, and 3 of 
astrocytes after SCI. In the acute phase after SCI, whereas populations of Clusters 1 and 4 are significantly reduced, those of Clusters 2 and 
3 are significantly increased. And this may lead to enhanced oxidative stress and inflammatory responses in the SC. In the subacute phase 
after SCI, the population of Cluster 1 is significantly increased. And this may promote regeneration of the SC. In the intermediate phase after 
SCI, the population of Cluster 2 is significantly increased. And this may help maintain homeostasis in the SCI.

      |  19 of 21
HOU et al.
family, Lcn2, H2-­T23, Serping1, H2-­D1, Il1β, Tnf, and C3,38 
while A2 specifically expresses BDNF, Il6, Emp1, S100a10, 
Slc10a6, Lif, Clcf1, Thbs1/2, and Tm4sf1. Based on these 
specific genes expressed by A1 or A2, we analyzed the 
“helpful” or “harmful” functions of six RA subtypes.38,65,66 
We found that Clusters 3, 4, and 6 expressed the A2 spe-
cific genes at the acute and subacute time after SCI, for 
example, Clusters 3 and 4 expressed S100a10 and Thbs1, 
Cluster 6 expressed Tm4sf1. However, at the intermediate 
time after SCI, Clusters 3 and 4 highly expressed C1q and 
H2-­D1 which were A1-­specific genes. Similarly, Clusters 
1, 2, 5, 9 also expressed Thbs1 at the acute time after SCI, 
so they tend to type A2 astrocytes. Then, Clusters 1, 2, 5, 
9 highly expressed Lcn2, and H2-­D1 after the acute time 
after SCI, so these cells were identified as type A1 astro-
cytes. Clusters 7 and 11 cells were considered type A2 
astrocytes due to their high expression of Cryab which 
has been shown to inhibit neuroinflammation and pro-
tect cells from apoptosis by inhibiting caspase activity.61 
Although we have classified these six subtypes into A1 
and A2 astrocytes, all of them performed functions neces-
sary for wound healing during SCI, for example, Cluster1 
and Clusters 2, 5, 9 cells performed the clearance of neu-
rotoxic cell debris and cytotoxic. Some previous studies 
targeting astrocytes of SCI have disrupted the early pro-
tective functions of astrocytes and thus failed to repair the 
SCI tissue.67
In summary, based on single-­cell RNA transcriptome 
data, we found and identified six types of astrocyte sub-
types, including naïve astrocytes (Cluster 4, Atp1a2+), as-
trocytes for clearance of necrotic tissue (Cluster1, Gpr84+), 
stress-­activated astrocytes (Cluster 3, S100a4+), Gfap-­low 
astrocytes (Clusters 2, 5 and 9, G0s2+/C3+), proliferating 
astrocytes (Cluster 6, Tm4sf1+/Gfap+), OPCs-­derived as-
trocytes (Clusters 7 and 11, Cryab+/Gss+) (Figures  8H 
and 9). Although the functions of these astrocytes in each 
cluster have been analyzed, direct detection of function in 
these astrocyte populations will be required in the future, 
using genetic methods or other appropriate tools. These 
data can not only analyze the heterogeneity of astro-
cytes but also provide a preliminary understanding of the 
functional bias of astrocytes and determine the A1 or A2 
type of astrocytes from the dimension of gene expression 
(Figures 8H and 9). These new classifications may help to 
explain the role of astrocytes in the pathological process 
of SCI.
