===== Hutson2019_NatRevNeuro_SCI+review =====
Acquired spinal cord injury (SCI) has a variety of causes, 
including traumatic events and inflammatory, infective, 
vascular, neoplastic or degenerative processes. The most 
common and severe injuries to the spinal cord are trau-
matic in nature. Traumatic injuries to the spinal column 
typically lead to the fracture of vertebral bodies, which 
become anteriorly dislocated to compress the spinal 
parenchyma, leading to variable degrees of permanent 
neurological dysfunction. Neurological impairments 
include motor, sensory and autonomic (sexual, urinary, 
cardiovascular and intestinal) dysfunction, the sever-
ity of which depends on the degree of damage and the 
spinal level at which the injury occurs. Most patients 
affected by SCI are young and are part of the workforce, 
so their long-​term disability imposes a high psychologi-
cal and financial burden. Therefore, the identification of 
disease-​modifying interventions for SCI is a high priority.
Immediately following spinal trauma, axonal and cel-
lular damage, along with haemorrhagic and ischaemic 
changes, cause breakdown of the blood–CNS barrier, 
leading to infiltration of immune cells such as neutro-
phils and macrophages1. Axonal and cellular membrane 
disruption, myelin debris, oedema and inflammation 
dominate the initial post-​injury phase, which is fol-
lowed by proliferation and activation of microglia and 
astrocytes. These cells secrete a range of factors that lead 
to resolution of the acute injury response, wound heal-
ing, and scar and cavity formation1. Neurological disa-
bility and failed recovery result from disruption of spinal 
connectivity due to axonal and cellular damage, followed 
by regenerative failure and limited neuroplasticity. Loss 
of connectivity of the damaged spinal circuitry is com-
mon to all types of spinal pathology, so any intervention 
that aims to restore lost connectivity could in principle 
be applied to the whole range of spinal disorders.
Current treatment for acute SCI is limited to surgi-
cal decompression and intravenous high-​dose methyl­
prednisolone, the clinical efficacy of which remains 
unclear2. Anti-​inflammatory or neuroprotective strate-
gies have so far failed to make an impact on the course 
of the disease and on clinical outcomes2. Brain–machine 
interfaces and epidural stimulation show considerable 
promise for recovery of function after SCI3,4, although 
these strategies harness the function of spared fibres 
and bypass the injury site rather than eliciting biological 
repair. Our increased understanding of the mechanisms 
that control neuroplasticity at the axonal and synaptic 
levels is providing novel opportunities for targeted thera­
peutic approaches to promote biological repair and 
functional recovery in both acute and chronic SCI.
The translational landscape in spinal 
cord injury: focus on neuroplasticity 
and regeneration
Thomas H. Hutson and Simone Di Giovanni   *
Abstract | Over the past decade, we have witnessed a flourishing of novel strategies to enhance 
neuroplasticity and promote axon regeneration following spinal cord injury , and results from 
preclinical studies suggest that some of these strategies have the potential for clinical translation. 
Spinal cord injury leads to the disruption of neural circuitry and connectivity , resulting in 
permanent neurological disability. Recovery of function relies on augmenting neuroplasticity 
to potentiate sprouting and regeneration of spared and injured axons, to increase the strength 
of residual connections and to promote the formation of new connections and circuits. 
Neuroplasticity can be fostered by exploiting four main biological properties: neuronal intrinsic 
signalling, the neuronal extrinsic environment, the capacity to reconnect the severed spinal cord 
via neural stem cell grafts, and modulation of neuronal activity. In this Review , we discuss 
experimental evidence from rodents, nonhuman primates and patients regarding interventions 
that target each of these four properties. We then highlight the strengths and challenges of 
individual and combinatorial approaches with respect to clinical translation. We conclude 
by considering future developments and providing views on how to bridge the gap between 
preclinical studies and clinical translation.
Division of Neuroscience, 
Department of Brain 
Sciences, Imperial College 
London, London, UK.
*e-​mail: s.di-​giovanni@
imperial.ac.uk
https://doi.org/10.1038/ 
s41582-019-0280-3
www.nature.com/nrneurol
Reviews
732 | DECEMBER 2019 | volume 15	

Neuroplasticity is the process whereby axons and 
synapses adapt to the continuously changing cellular 
environment, encompassing their electrical, metabolic 
and signalling requirements, to cope with the demands 
of neuronal activity and connectivity within established 
and novel circuits. After SCI, various forms of axon 
growth can occur, including collateral sprouting of 
spared and injured axons, structural synaptic remodel-
ling and long-​distance axon regeneration5, all of which 
are forms of neuroplasticity. Axon regeneration is the 
re-growth of transected axons across a lesion site 
towards their original synaptic targets, whereas other 
forms of axonal sprouting and synaptic remodelling 
result in circuit reorganization; these processes can all 
lead to restoration of function. Autocrine and paracrine 
signalling from local and long-​distance connections, 
as well as immune signals, help to shape neuroplastic­
ity and the regenerative response following an injury6. 
Nervous system injury presents particular challenges 
with regard to neuroplasticity potential as it generates 
unprecedented metabolic demands, inflammation, and 
hostile glial signalling and cellular environments.
Although few axons spontaneously regenerate for 
long distances owing to substantial physical and molec-
ular barriers, studies have shown that following SCI 
limited spontaneous sprouting does occur and can 
lead to modest motor and sensory improvements7,8. 
Moreover, cortical neuroplasticity can result in anatom-
ical reorganization of the primary motor cortex after 
SCI9–14. Various types of limited spinal plasticity have 
also been reported. For example, lesioned supraspinal 
corticospinal tract (CST) axons can sprout and form 
novel connections with spared supraspinal or unin-
jured propriospinal neurons15–17. In addition, spared CST 
fibres in the ventral column can exhibit compensatory 
collateral sprouting18,19, and uninjured propriospinal 
neurons can undergo synaptic rearrangement and col-
lateral sprouting following loss of supraspinal inputs20. 
However, the majority of axon sprouting that occurs is 
not functionally beneficial and can result in spasticity, 
dysreflexia and neuropathic pain21. The different forms 
of spontaneous repair are illustrated in Fig. 1.
Neuroplasticity has been investigated and enhanced 
in both preclinical animal models and patients with SCI, 
with neurorehabilitative, neurochemical and electrical 
stimulation approaches leading to measurable degrees of 
neurological recovery. Successful robust axon regenera-
tion remains rare, however, and significant restoration of 
neural circuits across the lesion remains a challenge22,23. 
In addition, several limitations of preclinical animal 
models confound data interpretation for translation to 
humans (Box 1).
Four main variables control neuroplasticity and the 
regenerative potential following SCI: neuronal intrin-
sic signalling, the neuronal extrinsic environment, the 
capacity to reconnect the severed spinal cord via neu-
ral stem cell (NSC) grafts, and modulation of neuronal 
activity. In this Review, we discuss preclinical and clin-
ical evidence supporting a role for the manipulation of 
each of these variables in augmenting neuroplasticity 
and sensorimotor recovery, and we consider how the 
gap that currently exists between preclinical studies and 
clinical translation might be narrowed.
Neuronal intrinsic signalling
In preclinical studies of SCI, neuronal intrinsic signalling 
has been manipulated by targeting the following com-
ponents: the growth cone and cytoskeleton to prevent 
growth cone collapse; axonal signalling, translation and 
protein synthesis; ion channels; and transcriptional 
and epigenetic mechanisms that reprogramme regener-
ative gene expression (Box 2). These mechanisms have 
been reviewed in detail elsewhere24,25.
Growth cone and cytoskeleton. Growth cones extend and 
retract in response to environmental cues via dynamic 
waves of microtubule polymerization and depolymeri-
zation. To foster extension of the growth cone following 
axonal injury and to counteract growth cone collapse, 
which typically occurs immediately after SCI, the bal-
ance must shift towards polymerization. Following 
spinal cord contusion injuries in rodents, promotion of 
microtubule polymerization via microtubule-​stabilizing 
agents, such as epothilone B or D, reduced growth cone 
collapse and axonal retraction and enhanced regenera-
tion of both ascending sensory and descending seroto­
nergic axons26,27. Systemic administration of epothilone B, 
which is used as an anticancer drug because of its anti-
mitotic properties, also reduced glial scarring, probably 
by inhibiting division of inflammatory cells and astro-
cytes following SCI28. Similar observations were reported 
when another anticancer microtubule-​stabilizing drug, 
taxol, was administered systemically following SCI29.
Both epothilone B and taxol were able to enhance 
some level of sensorimotor recovery in mice after a 
dorsal hemisection26 and rats after a contusive SCI29. 
However, in another study in a similar rat SCI model, 
using a comparable dose and systemic delivery, taxol did 
not promote functional recovery or serotonergic axonal 
sprouting30. A further study found that epothilone B 
could exacerbate the cytotoxic inflammatory response 
and limit, rather than facilitate, functional recovery31. 
Key points
• Spinal cord injury (SCI) is a complex pathological condition and although several 
therapeutic approaches have shown potential in preclinical studies, few have 
progressed to clinical trials.
• Understanding the spatial and temporal changes in transcription and chromatin 
accessibility in selected neuronal subpopulations after SCI could help identify key 
proteins that orchestrate specific changes in neuroplasticity.
• The use of chondroitinase ABC and anti-​NogoA treatment to reduce inhibitory 
signalling in the neuronal extrinsic environment after SCI has shown promise in terms 
of promoting axon sprouting and recovery.
• Unprecedented long-​distance axon regeneration, cell replacement and relay 
formation have been achieved using spinal cord-​derived neural stem cell grafts 
combined with growth factors.
• Neuromodulation strategies including electrical epidural stimulation and brain–
machine interfaces have demonstrated impressive improvements in voluntary motor 
function, and wireless systems will further improve the clinical utility of these 
strategies.
• Combining mechanism-​based biological strategies with targeted technological 
interventions to augment neuroplasticity, followed by rehabilitation to direct circuit 
reorganization, could facilitate clinically meaningful recovery after SCI.

Reviews
	
 volume 15 | DECEMBER 2019 | 733

Although the fact that these drugs are already used 
in the clinic to treat cancer makes them attractive, 
contro­versial results in animal models and cumulative 
dose-​dependent toxicity, including bone marrow, gastro-
intestinal and neurotoxic adverse effects32, restricts their 
potential for use in patients with chronic SCI.
Other approaches to limit growth cone collapse 
include pharmacological inhibition of the small GTPase 
RhoA, a signalling molecule that is localized at the 
growth cone. RhoA regulates intracellular cytoskeleton 
dynamics and mediates growth cone collapse and axon 
retraction through the second messenger Rho kinase 
(ROCK) and its downstream targets33–35. In rodent 
models of SCI, specific inactivation of RhoA by the 
bacterial toxin C3 transferase resulted in sprouting and 
regeneration of CST fibres and functional recovery36–38. 
After dorsal hemisection of the spinal cord, inhibition of 
ROCK using the synthetic compound Y-27632 enhanced 
sprouting and regeneration of CST axons and promoted 
functional improvements in hindlimb locomotion36,38. 
However, delayed treatment using fasudil, another 
ROCK inhibitor, did not enhance regeneration or func-
tional recovery, suggesting that ROCK inhibition is only 
useful for treating acute SCI39.
Axonal signalling, translation and protein synthesis. 
Axonal translation and protein synthesis at the growth 
cone have undergone intensive investigation recently 
(reviewed elsewhere40). An intriguing finding was the 
identification of active protein synthesis mediated by 
mechanistic target of rapamycin (mTOR) in the axon 
and growth cone41. mTOR is a serine/threonine protein 
kinase that stimulates ribosomal protein translation by 
inhibiting 4EBP1, an important repressor of translation 
initiation42. Phosphatase and tensin homologue (PTEN) 
is a negative regulator of mTOR, and genetic deletion or 
silencing of this molecule was shown to promote axonal 
regeneration of the CST in the mouse after a spinal cord 
hemisection43,44, even when the deletion occurred 1 year 
after SCI45.
These studies raise the possibility that further 
research into these pathways will yield future therapies 
for chronic SCI. However, although impressive, the 
degree of CST regeneration differed between studies. 
A much more robust regenerative phenotype that was 
associated with functional recovery after CST lesioning 
in mice was achieved by the combined genetic deletion 
of Pten and the cytokine signalling inhibitor suppres-
sor of cytokine signalling 3 (Socs3). This intervention 
led to substantial axon sprouting in the CST, which 
was responsible for improvements in locomotion and 
motor skills46. Gene therapy-​mediated silencing of these 
two genes might be an interesting future approach to 
enhance axonal regeneration and functional recovery 
in clinical SCI. However, more experimental evidence in 
contusion SCI models as well as in nonhuman primates 
is required, and concerns that the deletion of a tumour 
suppressor such as PTEN could lead to tumour growth 
remain a major limitation on the path to translation.
Insulin-​like growth factor 1 (IGF1), which controls 
both signalling and protein synthesis, has an important 
role in controlling the regenerative potential of neurons. 
Co-​delivery of pro-​inflammatory osteopontin and IGF1 
to the sensorimotor cortex promoted axonal regener-
ation and sprouting of the CST as well as recovery of 
skilled forelimb function after SCI in mice47. By contrast, 
systemic pharmacological inhibition of IGF1 receptor 
signalling after a dorsal hemisection in mice restricted 
the regenerative sprouting of raphespinal serotonergic 
fibres and locomotor recovery48. IGF1 overexpression is

cord injury. This schematic shows the different forms 
of anatomical neuroplasticity and regeneration that 
can occur spontaneously following spinal cord injury 
(SCI), including plasticity and reorganization of injured 
corticospinal neurons in the cortex (1); sprouting of 
injured corticospinal axons onto spared propriospinal 
neurons (2); sprouting of uninjured propriospinal neurons (3); 
and sprouting of uninjured corticospinal axons, which 
synapse with motor neurons below the injury site (4).

734 | DECEMBER 2019 | volume 15	

known to promote mTOR signalling and to affect pro-
tein translation. However, IGF1 also influences multiple 
signalling pathways that have not been systematically 
investigated in SCI models. In a pilot clinical study to 
address synaptic deficits in children with a monogenic 
form of autism, stimulation of IGF1 signalling was 
shown to be safe, tolerable and effective49. On the basis of 
these findings, IGF1 stimulation seems to be a promising 
avenue to explore in clinical trials for SCI, although the 
risk of developing tumours might again limit its clinical 
potential.
Ion channels. Approaches that manipulate synaptic and 
neuronal activity by modulating ion channel function 
have shown promise for increasing axon regenera-
tion and sprouting, synaptic plasticity and functional 
recovery. Deletion of the developmentally regulated 
gene Cacna2d2, which encodes the α2δ2 voltage-​
gated calcium channel subunit, promoted neurite 
outgrowth in vitro50. More importantly from a clinical 
perspective, in vivo pharmacological inhibition of α2δ2 
with the anticonvulsant drug pregabalin enhanced axon 
regeneration in adult mice after SCI50. Pregabalin is 
already used as a treatment for several neurological dis-
orders and could represent a novel regenerative strategy 
for SCI. The preclinical evidence is further reinforced by 
clinical data showing that early administration of gaba­
pentinoids, anticonvulsant agents similar to pregabalin, 
promoted motor recovery after SCI51.
Following a small-​molecule screening approach, 
genetic and pharmacological stimulation of KCC2, a 
neuronal specific chloride potassium cotransporter that 
is known to mediate synaptic inhibition, neuroprotection 
and neuroplasticity52, was found to promote relay circuit 
formation and functional sensorimotor recovery around 
a staggered spinal cord lesion in mice53. This finding 
highlighted KCC2 agonists as potential promising treat-
ments for promoting functional recovery after SCI and 
warrants further investigation in more clinically relevant 
SCI models to assess the potential for clinical translation.
Transcriptional and epigenetic mechanisms. Accumu­
lating experimental evidence suggests that transcrip-
tional and epigenetic programmes are important 
determinants of the ability of injured axons to grow, 
regenerate, sprout or form new synapses following 
injury54–56. To identify regenerative transcription factors, 
researchers have analysed transcription factor activity or 
expression during development, in regenerative organ-
isms and following injury to the mammalian PNS, which 
has a greater regenerative capacity than the mature CNS. 
This approach led to the identification of several candi-
date transcription factors for genetic or pharmacological 
manipulation to enhance axonal sprouting, regeneration 
and functional recovery after SCI.
Adeno-​associated virus (AAV)-mediated cortical 
overexpression of an active form of the developmentally 
regulated Krüppel-​like factor 7 (KLF7) promoted axonal 
sprouting in the CST after pyramidotomy or beyond 
the lesion site after a cervical hemisection57. However, the 
intervention failed to promote functional recovery. A sub-
sequent study in a mouse thoracic spinal contusion model 
showed that AAV-​mediated KLF7 overexpression in the 
dorsal thoracic spinal cord promoted propriospinal axonal 
plasticity and formation of synapses with motor neurons 
below the lesion site in the spinal cord58. These effects were 
associated with improved functional motor recovery.
The SOX family is another developmentally regulated 
class of transcription factors. AAV-​mediated SOX11 
overexpression was shown to promote sprouting of CST 
axons in the cervical spinal cord after pyramidotomy, 
as well as axon regeneration after cervical spinal cord 
hemisection59. However, impairment of forelimb func-
tion was also observed, raising the possibility of neuro-
logical dysfunction owing to aberrant axonal sprouting. 
The possibility that regenerative growth leads to neuro-
logical impairment rather than recovery is an important 
consideration when translating novel interventions into 
the clinic and emphasizes the importance of achieving 
consistent behavioural outcomes in preclinical models 
of SCI before initiating clinical trials.
The tumour suppressor p53 is also a developmentally 
regulated transcription factor, and AAV-​mediated over-
expression of this protein promoted CST sprouting after 
a spinal cord dorsal hemisection in mice60. An alterna-
tive approach is to activate p53 by inhibiting its interac-
tion with the E3 ubiquitin–protein ligase MDM2, which 
limits p53 activity and sequesters it in the cytoplasm61. 
Delivery of nutlin 3, a pharmacological antagonist of 
the MDM2–p53 interaction, promoted CST and raphe­
spinal sprouting following a thoracic spinal cord dorsal 
Box 1 | Limitations of preclinical studies
Preclinical studies to evaluate axon regeneration and functional recovery after spinal 
cord injury (SCI) have several limitations that could confound data interpretation.
Sample sizes
The numbers of animals used in behavioural studies is often insufficient to account for 
the high intrinsic variability of these studies.
Heterogeneity of SCI models
Variations in the degree of axonal injury, and in the ensuing glial and immune response, 
can influence axonal regeneration and functional recovery. Interspecies heterogeneity 
in the cellular response to SCI has been observed; for example, rats develop 
pronounced scar tissue and cavitation, similar to that seen in humans, whereas the 
mouse spinal cord is much less prone to cavity formation.
Interspecies variations in neuroanatomy
The main component of the corticospinal tract (CST) lies in the ventral part of the dorsal 
funiculus of the spinal cord in rats and mice but in the lateral funiculus in nonhuman 
primates and humans. Locomotion and fine motor skills rely heavily on the CST in humans 
but are much more dependent on alternative motor pathways such as raphespinal and 
reticulospinal tracts in rodents and, to a certain extent, in nonhuman primates162,163.
Age
As neuroplasticity declines with ageing, the age of the animals must be taken into 
consideration when comparing outcomes between studies.
Inbreeding
The immune system is essential for the injury response and has an increasingly 
recognized role in neuroplasticity and axonal regeneration164. Inbred mouse strains 
living in relatively sterile conditions in animal houses will have a dramatically different 
immune system from humans who are exposed to a large variety of pathogens.
Animal housing
The environment in which laboratory animals are housed is relatively impoverished in 
terms of environmental stimuli compared with their natural habitat. The level of 
environmental enrichment dramatically affects neuronal activity, which is crucial for 
neuroplasticity, regeneration and recovery after SCI77.
NATuRE REvIEWS | NEurOLOgy
Reviews
	
 volume 15 | DECEMBER 2019 | 735

hemisection in mice48. Importantly, nutlin 3 treatment 
also promoted functional motor recovery of the hind-
limbs48. Mechanistically, p53 activation led to the neu-
ronal induction of several regeneration-​associated genes 
(RAGs). In addition, activation of p53 reduced the pro-
liferation of astrocytes and macrophages and was asso-
ciated with reduction of the glial scar and of macrophage 
infiltration into the injured spinal cord48.
The transcription factor cAMP-​responsive element-​
binding protein (CREB) has been shown to be required 
for developmentally regulated axonal elongation62. CREB 
activation occurs via phosphorylation mediated by the 
cAMP–protein kinase A signalling pathway62,63. Several 
studies have shown that AAV-​mediated overexpression of 
active CREB or elevation of cAMP levels via the phospho­
diesterase 4 inhibitor rolipram can limit growth cone 
collapse and enhance regeneration of sensory axons fol-
lowing a spinal cord hemisection in mice64,65. More impor-
tantly, systemic delivery of rolipram after contusive SCI in 
rats resulted in enhanced locomotor recovery66. However, 
a phase I clinical trial of rolipram treatment in patients 
with multiple sclerosis was terminated prematurely 
because the drug was poorly tolerated, with an increase 
in the brain inflammatory response being observed67.
Signal transducer and activator of transcription 3 
(STAT3) is a transcription factor that is required for cell 
growth, survival and differentiation during neural devel-
opment68, and manipulation of this protein can directly 
affect neuroplasticity. AAV-​mediated overexpression of 
STAT3 increased collateral sprouting of sensory axons 
after a spinal dorsal column lesion in mice69. After 
pyramidotomy in mice, STAT3 overexpression pro-
moted collateral sprouting of lesioned and intact CST 
axons, thereby inducing forelimb motor recovery70.
The limited effects of transcription factor manipula-
tion on neuroplasticity and functional recovery might 
relate to the poor accessibility of transcription factors 
to active gene regulatory regions. Modulation of the 
epigenetic environment provides the possibility to 
both facilitate transcription factor accessibility to gene 
regulatory regions and orchestrate broad changes in 
gene expression. Epigenetic modifications include his-
tone acetylation, the extent of which reflects a balance 
between the activities of histone acetyltransferases and 
histone deacetylases (HDACs)71; DNA methylation, 
which is controlled by DNA methyltransferase and DNA 
demethylase; and DNA hydroxymethylation, which is 
controlled by the TET methylcytosine dioxygenase 
family72. Studies in dorsal root ganglia (DRG) neurons 
following peripheral versus central axonal injury have 
shown that in situations where regeneration is possi-
ble, such as in peripheral nerve injuries, histones are 
preferentially acetylated at active gene promoters of 
various RAGs73,74. This process occurs via post-​injury 
calcium-​dependent nuclear export of HDAC5 (ref.75) 
or via extracellular signal-​regulated kinase-​dependent 
phosphorylation of the histone acetyltransferase PCAF73. 
Similarly, following peripheral injury, some of these 
gene promoters display reduced or enhanced DNA 
methylation or hydroxymethylation, respectively76.
Epigenetic enzymes can be manipulated with pharma­
cological compounds or by gene editing to promote the 
expression of RAGs and regenerative signalling. In a 
mouse model, systemic pharmacological inhibition of 
class I HDACs with the compound MS-275 led to a 
reduction in the retraction of sensory axons after cer-
vical SCI, but no axons were found past the lesion bor-
ders74. A potential mechanism underlying these effects 
is the increased acetylation of several RAG promoters 
occupied by the histone acetyltransferase p300 and the 
transcription factor Smad1 in DRGs. In another study, 
AAV-​mediated overexpression of PCAF in DRGs pro-
moted axon regeneration into and past a spinal dor-
sal hemisection lesion in mice73. PCAF was found to 
acetylate the promoters of several RAGs, thereby driving 
their expression in sensory neurons.
We recently discovered that housing mice in an 
enriched environment induces neuroplasticity and 
functional recovery after SCI via activity-mediated 
CREB-​binding protein (CBP)-dependent histone acety­
lation77. In a rat model, we found that delivery of TTK21, 
a small-​molecule activator of CBP acetyltransferase, at 
clinically relevant times after a severe spinal cord con-
tusion injury promoted regeneration and sprouting of 
sensory and motor axons through epigenetic reprogram-
ming. CBP activation also enhanced synaptic plasticity, 
as indicated by a profusion of vesicular glutamate trans-
porter 1-positive presynaptic terminals in the proximity 
of motor neurons and improvements in both sensory 
and motor functions77. These findings could open up 
new avenues for therapeutic developments in clinical 
SCI, and an interesting next step would be to investi-
gate the potential of CBP activation, in association with 
neuro­rehabilitation, to achieve neuroplasticity and 
recovery after SCI in nonhuman primates.
Transcription factor manipulation has so far achieved 
limited efficacy in terms of recovery after SCI. As pre-
viously reviewed78, finding the right combination of 
transcription factors with the help of bioinformatic and 
computational approaches might allow us to further 
Box 2 | Targeting neuronal intrinsic and extrinsic mechanisms
Intrinsic mechanisms
• The microtubule stabilizer epothilone B promotes sensorimotor recovery after spinal 
cord injury (SCI) through a dual mechanism comprising enhancement of axonal 
regeneration and reduction of astrocyte proliferation and scar formation.
• Enhancement of insulin-​like growth factor 1 signalling within neurons leads to axonal 
regeneration and sprouting and sensorimotor functional recovery after SCI.
• Activation of cAMP-​responsive element-​binding protein (CREB) by the small molecule 
TTK21 results in improved sensorimotor recovery after SCI by enhancing axonal 
regeneration and sprouting of sensory and motor tracts, as well as promoting spinal 
circuit reorganization.
Extrinsic mechanisms
• Antagonistic antibodies to NogoA have been shown to promote axonal regeneration 
and sprouting and functional recovery in several models of rodent and nonhuman 
primate SCI by inhibiting Nogo receptor growth-​inhibitory signalling.
• Biochemical or viral vector-​mediated delivery of chondroitinase ABC to the injury site 
after SCI promotes axonal sprouting and synaptic plasticity in sensory and motor 
tracts. Chondroitinase ABC treatment has resulted in sensorimotor recovery in 
several rodent and nonhuman primate models of SCI.
• Protein tyrosine phosphatase σ antagonism promotes regeneration of the 
corticospinal tract and sprouting of serotonergic axons to promote respiratory, 
urinary and locomotor recovery following SCI.
www.nature.com/nrneurol
Reviews
736 | DECEMBER 2019 | volume 15	

enhance neuroplasticity as well as functional recovery 
following SCI. Cell-​specific and unbiased genome-​wide 
analysis of epigenetic and transcription factor signal-
ling, along with mechanistic studies, will be needed to 
better target epigenetic and transcriptional regenerative 
mechanisms following axonal injury.
Combining epigenetic with transcription factor 
manipulation might make an otherwise inaccessible 
transcriptional environment accessible, thereby facilitat-
ing the expression of genes encoding factors that induce 
regeneration and functional recovery. Figure 2 illus-
trates the transcriptional and epigenetic pathways that 
can potentially be targeted to improve axonal sprouting 
and regeneration. This approach will need to be explored 
in the preclinical setting before clinical translation can 
be considered.
The neuronal extrinsic environment
Following SCI, reactive astrocytes, oligodendrocytes, 
microglia and other immune cells from the bloodstream, 
as well as the factors that they secrete, contribute to 
growth cone collapse and axonal retraction via the con-
vergence of multiple receptor-​dependent signalling path-
ways. These mechanisms have been extensively reviewed 
elsewhere79. In this section, we focus on experimental 
evidence that manipulation of the neuronal extrinsic 
environment and its associated signalling can enhance 
neuroplasticity and functional recovery after SCI (Box 2).
Chondroitin sulfate proteoglycans. The upregulation of 
chondroitin sulfate proteoglycans (CSPGs) in the extra-
cellular matrix (ECM) by reactive astrocytes, fibroblasts, 
macrophages and NG2-positive glia after SCI presents 
an important barrier to neuroplasticity. Following an 
injury, these cells accumulate around the lesion epi­centre 
and at more distant sites of denervation over weeks and 
months80, leading to the deposition of CSPGs which 
limit neuroplasticity, axonal sprouting, regeneration and 
recovery. CSPGs can bind to one of two neuronal recep-
tors, protein tyrosine phosphatase σ (PTPσ) or the Nogo 
receptor complex, to trigger an intracellular signalling 
cascade that activates RhoA, which is responsible for 
growth cone collapse and axonal retraction. CSPGs are 
also the main component of perineuronal nets (PNNs), 
which are found around neurons throughout the CNS 
and play an important role in synaptic stability and in 
limiting axonal sprouting.
Chondroitinase ABC (ChABC) degrades CSPG glyco­
saminoglycan chains, inhibits the interaction of CSPGs 
with their receptors79 and removes PNNs. In animal 
models of SCI, this enzyme enhanced axonal sprouting 
and synaptic plasticity, leading to recovery of function81,82. 
The potential of this approach for clini­cal translation 
was demonstrated in a recent study, in which ChABC 
treatment after a C7 spinal hemisection in nonhuman 
primates resulted in sprouting of CST axons below 
the injury and partial restoration of hand function, with 
no adverse effects83. However, ChABC is a bacterial 
enzyme with poor stability and is readily degraded, so 
con­tinuous delivery or repeated dosing over weeks to 
months is necessary to influence neuro­plasticity and 
axonal regeneration in the long term after SCI.
To address these limitations, an immune-​evasive dual 
vector system has been developed in which the ChABC 
is expressed under a doxycycline-​inducible regulatory 
switch, utilizing a chimeric transactivator designed to 
evade T cell recognition84. When this system was used 
following spinal contusion in adult rats, ChABC expres-
sion could be temporally controlled in a tightly regu-
lated fashion in both the short term (2.5 weeks) and the 
long term (8 weeks), resulting in improved locomotion 
and manual dexterity, respectively. The treatment led to 
increased neuroplasticity, as indicated by a profusion of 
vesicular glutamate transporter 1-positive presynaptic 
terminals in the spinal cord grey matter above and below 
the injury. This vector system represents a considerable 
preclinical advance towards clinical use, and is currently 
being tested in a nonhuman primate model of cervical 
SCI. However, the effects of ChABC on PNNs could be 
a cause for concern, as these structures are important 
for the stabilization of synapses and the maintenance of 
cellular signalling and are involved in numerous cellular 
functions as well as several pathological states85. A low 
level of basal ‘leaky’ ChABC expression could therefore 
affect cellular signalling, spinal plasticity and pain states 
in the long term.
The discovery of PTPσ as a specific receptor for 
CSPGs86 led to the development of a novel PTPσ antag­
onistic peptide that was found to inhibit CSPG signal-
ling after contusive SCI in rats. Systemic delivery of 
this peptide resulted in substantial serotonergic axon

Fig. 2 | Targeting of transcriptional and epigenetic pathways after spinal cord injury. 
The diagram shows an example of three interconnected molecular pathways involving 
the tumour suppressor p53, which functions as a transcription factor, and histone 
acetyltransferase KAT2B (PCAF) and cAMP-​responsive element-​binding protein  
(CREB)-binding protein (CBP)/p300, which function as transcriptional cofactors and 
histone acetyltransferases. These three factors have been shown to enhance axonal 
sprouting and regeneration after spinal cord injury by forming a transcriptional complex. 
They can be targeted with small molecules (green ovals) or gene therapy to increase  
the acetylation of histones, thereby providing a favourable transcriptional environment 
and enhancing the expression of regeneration-​associated genes (RAGs). AAV, adeno-​
associated virus; MDM2, E3 ubiquitin–protein ligase MDM2; STAT3, signal transducer 
and activator of transcription 3.
NATuRE REvIEWS | NEurOLOgy
Reviews
	
 volume 15 | DECEMBER 2019 | 737

sprouting and re-​innervation below the level of injury, 
which facilitated recovery of both locomotor and uri-
nary functions87. In line with these data, deletion of the 
PTPσ receptor increased the regeneration of CST axons 
after spinal cord dorsal column lesioning in mice88.
Although the use of ChABC to target the glial scar is 
a promising strategy, it has recently been demonstrated 
that astrocytic borders around a spinal lesion can sup-
port axon regeneration89. Astrocytes can also secrete 
neurotrophic factors and form bridges across the spi-
nal injured tissue that facilitate axonal growth90. The 
apparently contradictory roles of astrocytes after SCI 
are probably attributable to their complex post-​injury 
phenotypes, ranging from cells secreting growth factors 
to cells releasing growth inhibitory molecules91. No evi-
dence currently exists that CSPG degradation affects the 
complexity of the astrocyte phenotypes after injury, but 
the possibility should not be ruled out.
Myelin protein-​dependent axonal growth inhibitory 
signalling. Several myelin-​associated inhibitory (MAI) 
proteins have been identified, including myelin-​associated 
glycoprotein (MAG)92, oligodendrocyte myelin glyco-
protein (OMgp)93 and NogoA94,95. MAI proteins origi-
nate from oligodendrocytes and disrupted myelin, and 
they accumulate in the proximity of the spinal injury 
site. These proteins bind to the Nogo receptor complex 
(NgR1), triggering intracellular signalling that ultimately 
leads to growth cone collapse and inhibition of axonal 
sprouting and regeneration in the injured mammalian 
CNS. The MAI proteins and their signalling pathways 
have been reviewed in detail elsewhere96, and here we 
focus on preclinical and clinical evidence that MAI inhi-
bition can promote neuroplasticity and regeneration 
after SCI.
Blocking NogoA with antagonistic antibodies in the 
acute phase following spinal cord thoracic hemisection 
or transection in rodents and primates led to increased 
sprouting of corticospinal, rubrospinal and raphespinal 
axons from both lesioned and spared fibres97, and some 
long-​distance regeneration of CST axons was observed. 
However, research in more clinically relevant contusion 
injuries is lacking, and evidence that delayed application 
of NogoA antibody in rats with SCI is less effective than 
acute application98 suggests that the NogoA antagonism 
is most appropriate for early intervention after SCI. In 
antibody-​treated animals, increased neuroplasticity was 
typically paralleled by enhanced recovery of locomotion 
in terms of stepping and coordination99–102. Similarly, 
delivery of anti-​NogoA antibodies resulted in meaning-
ful improvements in skilled hand and finger movements 
in a model of cervical spinal hemisection in nonhuman 
primates103,104. In the latter two studies, sprouting of the 
contralateral intact CST was observed and was believed 
to underpin the functional recovery.
NgR1 antagonist peptide ligands have also been tested 
in preclinical models of SCI. The human sequence-​
derived soluble NgR1 decoy protein NgR1(310)-Fc, 
which blocks the binding of NogoA, MAG and OMgp 
to NgR1, enhanced sprouting of raphespinal axons and 
produced modest functional recovery after acute or 
chronic contusion SCI in rodents105.
A phase I trial of intrathecal delivery of an anti-​
NogoA antibody, ATI355, has been successfully com-
pleted in patients with acute SCI106. The treatment was 
initiated between 4 and 60 days post-​injury, and contin-
uous intrathecal ATI355 infusion was performed over 
periods ranging from 24 h to 28 days. ATI355 was shown 
to be well tolerated in humans and safety was success-
fully demonstrated, and a multicentre phase II clinical 
trial to test efficacy after acute SCI is now under way.
In a study published in 2017, sequential administra-
tion of a NogoA-​blocking antibody followed by loco­
motor training after spinal transection in rats resulted in 
significant improvements in locomotion and increased 
sprouting of corticospinal and serotonergic axons 
and synapse formation onto motor neurons107. However, 
when both treatments were given simultaneously, no 
synergistic effects were observed108. A translational 
approach can be envisaged, in which the combination 
of anti-​NogoA antibodies followed by neurorehabilita-
tion is used to first enhance axonal sprouting and then 
consolidate functional synaptic contacts to improve 
functional recovery.
Neural stem cell grafts
Several reviews have focused on the potential of various 
types of stem cells and cellular therapies to promote spi-
nal repair after injury109,110. Cell transplants can provide 
trophic support, neuroprotection and anti-​inflammatory 
effects, as well as forming permissive tissue bridges 
across the spinal lesion.
A phase I clinical trial demonstrated the efficacy of 
OPC1, an oligodendrocyte progenitor cell population 
derived from human embryonic stem cells, in patients 
with severe cervical SCI111. After injection into the injury 
site, the cells seemed to have engrafted, thereby limit-
ing cavitation and improving motor function, in 21 of 
22 patients. These findings have led the authors to seek 
permission for a randomized phase II clinical trial to fur-
ther substantiate efficacy and safety. In another phase I 
clinical trial, the human spinal cord-​derived NSC line 
NSI-566 was shown to be well tolerated and safe in 
four patients with thoracic SCI112. However, as stated 
by the authors, this study lacked a control group and 
was heavily underpowered. Interestingly, and perhaps 
surprisingly, Japan has recently approved the clinical 
use of intravenously injected mesenchymal stem cells 
for SCI following a phase I clinical trial including just 
13 patients. Concerns over the basis of the approval, 
including the lack of a double-​blinded, randomized 
controlled clinical trial, have led to speculation over the 
efficacy and safety of the treatment113.
In the rest of this section, we review the most recent 
advances in stem cell transplantation in SCI, high-
lighting strategies that have been shown to directly 
influence axonal regeneration, sprouting and recon-
nectivity. A specific focus will be on work that shows 
the capacity of stem cell grafts to integrate and connect 
within the exogenous network and circuitry as well as 
with the endogenous host neural circuits to form novel 
neuronal relays (Fig. 3).
The first clear anatomical suggestion that trans-
planted NSCs could extend axons long distances in the 
www.nature.com/nrneurol
Reviews
738 | DECEMBER 2019 | volume 15	

injured adult mammalian spinal cord and form relays 
with descending motor pathways came from the Fischer 
laboratory114. The researchers demonstrated the for-
mation of potential relays between neuronal and glial 
restricted precursors and descending pathways, which 
was associated with improved bladder and motor func-
tion. The same laboratory also showed that, in adult 
rats, grafting of neuronal and glial restricted progeni-
tors within a dorsal column lesion, combined with brain-​
derived neurotrophic factor expression from a lentiviral 
vector, resulted in bidirectional extension of axons up 
to 9 mm from the lesion site115. The lesions were only 
partial, however, leaving open the question of efficacy 
after more severe injuries.
In a later study from the Tuszynski laboratory, green 
fluorescent protein-​expressing NSCs were grafted with a 
cocktail of ten trophic factors into the lesion site 2 weeks 
after a complete transection of the rat spinal cord116. The 
cells showed long-​term survival and differentiated into 
mature neurons that received synaptic contacts from 
host axons. The grafted differentiated neurons were 
also able to form electrophysiologically active neuronal 
relays and to extend numerous axons for long distances 
both caudally and rostrally from the injury. Importantly, 
the authors also found modest but noticeable improve-
ments in hindlimb motor function in treated animals 
compared with controls. These improvements seemed 
to depend on the formation of new neuronal relays, as a 
re-​transection at the graft–host interface abolished the 
functional improvements.
In a subsequent paper, the same group demon-
strated that human induced pluripotent stem cells from 
a mature healthy individual differentiated into NSCs and 
could be grafted into a rat following a cervical lateral 
hemisection117. These cells survived and differentiated 
for over 3 months after transplantation and extended 
long axons throughout the neuraxis into the white mat-
ter, making synapses with host neurons. Importantly, 
host supraspinal motor neurons also extended axons 
into the graft, forming synapses with graft neurons.
The Tuszynski laboratory also provided the first 
demonstration that CST axons could regenerate into 
and beyond an NSC graft, both in a thoracic complete 
transection and in a more focal cervical CST lesion118. 
Following a cervical CST lesion, functional synapses 
were identified, as well as improvements in skilled fore-
limb function, reinforcing the translational potential 
of this approach. A key milestone was reached when 
NSC grafts were implanted 2 weeks after a more clin-
ically relevant cervical contusion injury in rats119. The 
NSCs were found to differentiate into neurons and glia 
and to extend axons for long distances both rostrally and 
caudally from the injury. Most importantly from a 
translational perspective, these axons formed synapses 
with host neurons and the grafted animals recovered 
appreciable forelimb motor function compared with the 
control groups.
Another step in the pathway to clinical translation 
came from a study in which human spinal cord-​derived 
NSCs were grafted into a cervical SCI site in a non­
human primate (Macaca mulatta). Grafts survived up to 
9 months after transplantation combined with immuno­
suppression, and the cells differentiated into mature 
neuronal and glial phenotypes. An impressive number 
of axons extended for long distances out of the grafts 
into the white matter and a percentage formed syn-
apses, including onto host target neurons. Importantly,

Fig. 3 | Formation of neuronal relays after spinal cord injury. The diagram depicts the 
different neuronal relays that can form between the host tissue and a neural stem cell 
graft after spinal cord injury. Descending corticospinal axons regenerate into the graft 
and make synaptic connections to grafted neurons (1). Descending subcortical neurons 
regenerate axons and make connections with grafted neurons (2). Grafted neurons 
extend axons into the host spinal cord caudal to the injury and make synaptic contacts 
with motor neurons (3). Grafted neurons extend ascending axons rostral to the injury and 
make synaptic contacts with host neurons (4). Grafted neurons extend axons into host 
tissue, making synaptic contacts with propriospinal neurons (5).
NATuRE REvIEWS | NEurOLOgy
Reviews
	
 volume 15 | DECEMBER 2019 | 739

the authors observed an improvement in forelimb func-
tion months after the grafts were implanted. This work 
opens up the exciting possibility that NSC grafts might 
promote functional recovery in humans after SCI120.
Two recent studies, again from the Tuszynski labora-
tory, demonstrated how NSC grafts implanted at a lesion 
site give rise to diverse spinal neuron populations and 
form correct organotypic domains in the spinal cord. 
Surprisingly, injured host motor and sensory axons 
regenerating into these grafts were able to navigate to 
appropriate target domains, suggesting that correct spi-
nal circuitry can be restored without the need for further 
exogenous guidance121,122.
Concerns have been raised that NSCs might migrate 
into undesired locations within or outside the neuro­
axis or remain undifferentiated for an extended period 
before becoming mutagenic and giving rise to cancer123 
(reviewed elsewhere109). The development of fibrin-​
based matrices in which the cells are embedded in a 
scaffold, or the use of alternative biomaterials with 
longer durability than fibrin, might limit these risks, 
which, admittedly, do not seem to be particularly well 
grounded considering the evidence from most preclin-
ical studies. Others have speculated that the cocktail of 
trophic factors delivered with the NSC grafts might lead 
to the development of neuropathic pain or other aber-
rant sensory symptoms, as has been observed after deliv-
ery of nerve growth factor into the dorsal spinal cord124. 
However, only one report so far has suggested the occur-
rence of aberrant pain or sensation as an adverse effect 
of NSC grafting125.
In our view, a crucial question that remains to be 
answered is whether grafted cells can form a sufficient 
number of functional connections in the right locations 
and are sustained in the long term to establish stable 
neuronal relays with the host tissue, as will be necessary 
to achieve substantial functional recovery in human SCI. 
Stem cell grafts alone are unlikely to be sufficient to max-
imize functional recovery, and we expect that combinato-
rial strategies combining NSC grafts with modulation of 
neuronal activity, neurorehabilitation and enhancement 
of intrinsic neuroplasticity will be required.
Neuromodulation
Most patients who have motor complete SCI according 
to American Spinal Injury Association criteria retain 
some spared descending connections, yet spinal excit-
ability is substantially disrupted, rendering intact spinal 
circuits below the injury nonfunctional. The principle 
behind electrical or pharmacological (monoamine) 
neuro­modulation (Box 3; Fig. 4) is to increase the base-
line level of spinal excitability so that low levels of spared 
supraspinal and/or proprioceptive input can surmount 
motor thresholds, resulting in voluntary motor func-
tion126–128. When neuromodulation is combined with 
motor-​based training, spinal networks can reorganize 
and re-​learn how to process cutaneous and proprio­
ceptive sensory information into motor output. The elici­-
ted activity-​dependent structural and synaptic plasticity 
is likely to involve contributions from spared proprio-
ceptive afferents, descending motor axons, interneurons 
and motor neurons4 and can result in modest improve-
ments in volitional motor function even in the absence 
of neuromodulation.
The widespread and long-​term use of neuromodu-
lation devices in the clinical setting is currently limited 
by a number of factors, including affordability, acces-
sibility and the expertise required for the operation of 
some of the equipment. Furthermore, durability might 
become an issue as implanted electrodes can cause tissue 
damage and gliosis around the implant site, resulting in 
deficits in recording and stimulation efficiency129. The 
development of biocompatible nanoelectrode arrays 
with increased flexibility and smaller size should help to 
minimize tissue damage while increasing the efficiency 
and longevity of the devices130–132.
Electrical epidural stimulation. A growing body of evi-
dence from both animal and human studies indicates 
that electrical epidural stimulation (EES), whereby elec-
trodes are surgically implanted onto the dorsal surface 
of the spinal cord, can improve both involuntary and 
volun­tary motor function4,133. EES of the lumbosacral 
spinal cord targets intact spinal circuitry that no longer 
Box 3 | Neuromodulatory technologies and approaches
Electrical epidural stimulation
To date, electrical epidural stimulation (EES), in association with neurorehabilitation, 
has had the greatest clinical impact of any neuromodulatory approach for the 
treatment of spinal cord injury (SCI). Electrodes are surgically implanted onto the 
dorsal surface of the spinal cord to stimulate intact spinal circuitry that no longer 
receives sufficient supraspinal input. In combination with activity-​based rehabilitation, 
EES promotes neurological recovery by enhancing neuroplasticity and circuit 
reorganization. Recently developed wireless systems are improving the clinical utility 
and spatiotemporal precision of EES.
Neurochemical stimulation
Clinically relevant neuromodulatory pharmacological agents, including serotonergic 
and dopaminergic receptor agonists, promote spinal circuit excitability and 
neuroplasticity when combined with electrical stimulation and/or neurorehabilitation 
after SCI. These agents provide excellent adjuvant treatments but are not effective as 
stand-​alone therapies.
Brain stimulation
Clinically feasible noninvasive brain stimulation approaches to treat SCI include 
transcranial direct current stimulation and transcranial magnetic stimulation, which 
stimulate neuroplasticity typically in spared pathways between the brain and spinal 
cord. These techniques have low spatiotemporal precision but are noninvasive, 
clinically accessible and comparatively inexpensive.
Brain–machine interfaces
Brain–machine interfaces record and decode motor signals from the brain to allow 
control of motor input and bypass a spinal cord lesion in injured patients and animals. 
These devices also seem to enhance activity-​dependent neuroplasticity. This approach 
is clinically feasible but substantial technological improvements are still required.
Optogenetics
Optogenetics refers to the modulation of neuronal activity by light-​sensitive ion 
channels known as opsins, which can be delivered to specific neuronal populations via 
viral vectors or transgenic technologies. Optogenetic devices have high spatiotemporal 
precision but require surgical implantation and are not clinically viable for the treatment 
of SCI at the present time.
Chemogenetics
Chemogenetics refers to the modulation of neuronal activity by the use of compounds 
that activate engineered receptor complexes expressed in selected neuronal 
populations, typically via promoter-​specific viral transduction. This approach has high 
spatial but low temporal precision, and is currently not clinically viable in patients 
with SCI.
www.nature.com/nrneurol
Reviews
740 | DECEMBER 2019 | volume 15	

receives sufficient supraspinal input. On its own, stim-
ulation at submotor threshold levels enabled patients 
diagnosed with motor and sensory complete lesions to 
process conceptual, auditory and visual inputs to elicit 
small voluntary muscle contractions126. However, when 
EES was combined with activity-​based training, patients 
gained voluntary contractions that persisted in the 
absence of stimulation, suggesting that neuroplasticity 
and circuit reorganization had been induced, thereby 
enhancing neurological recovery134.
Both theoretical and experimental evidence indicates 
that low-​frequency EES activates proprioceptive affer-
ents135–138, which are important for reorganizing spinal 
circuits and remodelling spared descending motor tracts 
to activate motor neurons9,139. Targeting of proprio-
ceptive circuits through the stimulation of individual 
posterior roots enables motor neuron pools in differ-
ent spinal segments to be spatiotemporally activated 
to coincide with a voluntary movement140. One study 
combined wireless real-​time spatiotemporal control of 
EES with overground locomotor training and a gravity 
assist system in three patients with chronic cervical SCI 
who displayed severe lower limb deficits or complete 
paralysis23. This strategy led to immediate voluntary 
control of walking with EES. Motor recovery continued 
to improve with rehabilitative training, to the extent that 
patients could walk or cycle outside the clinical environ-
ment with EES. Excitingly, after several months, patients 
regained volitional control of previously paralysed mus-
cles without EES. The authors hypothesized that this 
recovery might be due to spatiotemporal convergence 
of residual supraspinal pathways and proprioceptive cir-
cuits, leading to the activation of motor neuron pools23. 
Whether this remodelling was mediated solely via local 
spinal circuitry or whether it also involved facilitation of 
ascending sensory axons that modulated the plasticity 
of descending motor pathways in the brainstem and 
cortical motor areas is yet to be determined.
Transcutaneous stimulation, whereby electrodes are 
placed on the skin over the spine to stimulate dorsal roots 
and engage locomotor circuits, was shown to improve 
upper limb function in a patient with chronic incomplete 
SCI at the C3 level of the spinal cord141. When combined 
with training, transcutaneous stimulation aided lower 
limb motor control in five patients with chronic motor 
complete SCI127. Transcutaneous stimulation has the 
advantage of being noninvasive, inexpensive and read-
ily available in the clinic. However, it lacks the spatio­
temporal precision of EES and does not allow specific 
muscle groups to be activated during a step cycle.
Brain stimulation. Several clinical trials have used nonin-
vasive methods such as transcranial direct current stimu­
lation or transcranial magnetic stimulation to stimulate 
spared pathways between the brain and spinal cord in 
patients with SCI. When combined with motor training, 
these techniques can facilitate activity-​dependent plasti­
city in spared circuits and ultimately improve volitional 
motor function. Transcranial direct current stimulation 
was shown to promote hand function when delivered in 
combination with repetitive task training142. Transcranial 
magnetic stimulation was shown to enhance fine motor 
hand function in patients with tetraplegia and severe 
complete cervical SCI143–145. The noninvasive nature of 
these interventions, combined with a good safety pro-
file, low cost and good clinical availability, make them 
an attractive option for treatment after SCI. However, the 
duration of the clinical benefit directly stemming from 
these interventions remains unclear.
Preclinical studies have demonstrated that electrical 
stimulation of the cortex can lead to sprouting of spared 
descending CST axons, resulting in recovery of fine fore-
limb functions146,147, which was further improved when 
cortical stimulation was combined with neuromodulation 
of the spinal cord148,149.

Fig. 4 | Neuromodulatory approaches to restore function after spinal cord injury. 
The figure summarizes the roles of optogenetics and chemogenetics as well as transcranial 
magnetic stimulation in promoting cortical motor plasticity after spinal cord injury. 
Brain–machine interfaces and electrical epidural stimulation promote plasticity of 
proprioceptive afferents, and intrathecal injections of neuromodulatory drugs can 
enhance neuroplasticity in the spinal cord. Optogenetics and chemogenetics are 
experimental approaches that are currently limited to preclinical models, whereas 
epidural stimulation, transcranial magnetic stimulation and brain–machine interfaces  
are already being used in patients. Further development of all of these approaches will 
increase the range of possibilities for neuronal activity-​based repair strategies in the 
clinic. CNO, clozapine-​N-oxide.
NATuRE REvIEWS | NEurOLOgy
Reviews
	
 volume 15 | DECEMBER 2019 | 741

Brain–machine interfaces. Exciting developments in 
neuromodulation are occurring in the area of brain–
machine interfaces, which record and decode motor sig-
nals from the brain and use these signals to functionally 
and temporally control prosthetic robotic limbs, exo-
skeletons or electrodes to directly influence motor out-
put. The premise behind brain–machine interfaces is to 
bypass the injury site to allow the brain to regain control 
of an artificial limb150,151 or muscles below the injury152. 
However, when this approach has been combined with 
rehabilitative training, patients have also seen improve-
ments in voluntary motor control, suggesting that these 
devices facilitate activity-​dependent neuroplasticity 
in the brain and/or spinal cord, leading to functional 
neurological improvements3.
Brain–machine–spinal cord interfaces have been 
used to directly modulate the spinal cord to improve per-
formance on forelimb-​reaching tasks in macaques153–155. 
In comparison with continuous spinal stimulation, 
brain-​controlled modulation of the spinal cord enhanced 
long-​term recovery of locomotion in rats after a severe 
thoracic contusion SCI156.
Brain–machine interfaces require expensive, spe-
cialized equipment, as well as the expertise to install 
and operate these devices, thereby creating a barrier to 
their widespread use. Furthermore, to use the interface, 
patients are currently required to be tethered to the 
equipment. The introduction of wireless technology 
should facilitate the delivery and operation of brain–
machine interfaces and pave the way towards independ-
ent patient use outside the clinical setting. An exciting 
study in macaques, published in 2016, employed a wire-
less brain–machine–spinal cord interface to decode cor-
tical motor intentional signals into the spatiotemporal 
control of an epidural stimulator placed over the lum-
bar spinal cord, and demonstrated marked improve-
ments in weight-​bearing stepping after unilateral 
thoracic hemisection136.
Combinatorial approaches
The treatment of SCI presents clinicians with com-
plex and multifaceted hurdles that are unlikely to be 
surmounted by a single approach, and combinatorial 
therapies are a promising strategy to restore meaning-
ful functional recovery. Combining treatments that 
promote neuronal plasticity with rehabilitative training 
paradigms seems to be an effective approach. Following 
the induction of neuronal plasticity and axonal sprout-
ing, rehabilitative training is thought to facilitate the 
formation of appropriate connections.
A study in rats combined intrathecal ChABC treat-
ment with rehabilitation of manual dexterity or loco­
motor training. Both of these combinations led to 
increased axon sprouting in the CST, but only rats that 
received task-​specific rehabilitation showed improved 
manual dexterity; animals that received general loco-
motor rehabilitation were better at ladder walking but 
had worse skilled-​reaching abilities than rats that received 
no treatment157. These findings suggest that neuro­
rehabilitation can extend and consolidate functionally 
relevant neuroplasticity induced by ChABC but also 
underlines how inappropriately targeted rehabilitation 
paradigms might harness axonal and synaptic plas-
ticity in a way that negatively affects neurological 
function. In a subsequent rat study, delivery of ChABC 
and an anti-​NogoA antibody combined with delayed 
rehabilitative treadmill training was shown to signifi-
cantly increase axon sprouting and functional recov-
ery after a cervical partial SCI compared with either 
treatment alone158.
The timing of initiation of rehabilitative train-
ing paradigms when used in combination with other 
therapies seems to be important to promote func-
tional improvements. Several studies have shown that 
plasticity-​enhancing treatments should be administered 
before commencing rehabilitation training107,108,159. 
This approach allows sufficient time for axonal growth 
and sprouting to occur before the new synaptic connec-
tions are stabilized and strengthened with rehabilita-
tive training. Simultaneously combining therapies and 
training might induce competition for neural substrates 
between different axonal tracts, resulting in a negative 
functional outcome.
Cell-​based therapies have also been combined with 
rehabilitation. Following complete spinal cord transec-
tion in rats, additional improvements in stepping were 
observed when long-​term training was added to an 
olfactory ensheathing cell transplantation paradigm160. 
Another promising strategy involves the combination 
of neurochemical modulators such as serotonergic and 
dopaminergic receptor agonists with EES and treadmill 
training161. In rats, this approach led to a striking restora-
tion of voluntary control of locomotion after a full tran-
section that was achieved with staggered hemisections 
in the thoracic spinal cord.
Conclusions and perspectives
In this concluding section, we critically discuss the pros-
pects for translation of preclinical studies to clinical SCI. 
Key challenges and future prospects are summarized 
in Box 4.
Although substantial progress has been made in the 
field of SCI treatment over the past decade, with many 
preclinical studies demonstrating enhanced neuroplas-
ticity and therapeutic potential in animal models, the 
few strategies that have progressed to clinical trials have 
shown little or no efficacy with regard to improving 
functional recovery. This translation gap could be partly 
attributable to the limitations of our current preclini-
cal SCI models (Box 1). Replication of preclinical results 
in independent laboratories, using double-​blinded, 
rando­mized controlled experimental designs, and in 
non­human primate models might help to improve trans-
lation to the clinic. However, single therapies that show 
efficacy in rodents might not be sufficient to augment 
neuroplasticity and recovery in humans, and combina-
torial strategies involving the four ‘pillars’ of neuroplas-
ticity discussed in this Review are likely to be required. 
Care needs to be taken to identify and understand the 
complex interactions between therapies and the best 
temporal window when combining different strategies. 
The ideal combination should work synergistically to 
overcome the multiple hurdles associated with SCI, 
resulting in clinically relevant functional improvements.
www.nature.com/nrneurol
Reviews
742 | DECEMBER 2019 | volume 15	

We believe that the most promising preclinical strat-
egy to promote long-​distance regeneration of axons 
in the spinal cord has come from recent studies in the 
Tuszynski laboratory, which used human spinal cord-​
derived NSC grafts combined with a cocktail of growth 
factors. This strategy has shown consistent efficacy in 
clinically relevant contusion models in both rodents and 
nonhuman primates. As well as producing impressive 
axon regeneration from the graft, this approach has the 
potential to promote neuroprotection and remyelina-
tion. Importantly, these studies have demonstrated the 
feasibility of cell replacement with phenotypically appro-
priate neurons that allow topographically correct relay 
formation with disrupted host axons. Although some 
safety concerns remain, the degree of axon growth is 
unprecedented and offers clear translational potential.
Multiple preclinical studies in different institutions, 
using rodent and nonhuman primate models, have 
shown that both anti-​NogoA antibodies and ChABC 
treatment can reliably promote axon sprouting and 
functional improvements without substantial detrimen-
tal effects. Approaches that tackle the inhibitory CNS 
environment are likely to be an important component of 
combinatorial strategies to improve meaningful recovery.
In our view, manipulation of neuronal intrinsic 
regenerative mechanisms does not show translational 
prospects at the present time. However, this approach 
still has tremendous potential to enhance neuroplasticity 
in specific neuronal circuits. To this end, we believe that 
it will be vital to understand how the transcriptional and 
epigenetic chromatin environments change after CNS 
injury in selected neuronal subpopulations. Recently 
developed single-​cell multiomics approaches are likely 
to aid this effort. Understanding the spatial and temporal 
changes in chromatin accessibility could lead to the iden-
tification of key proteins such as chromatin organizers or 
pioneer transcription factors, which are involved in chro-
matin remodelling and transcription initiation. These 
proteins could be temporally controlled to orchestrate 
specific changes in regenerative gene expression.
Last but not least, we would like to highlight the 
translational potential of combining biological, engi-
neering and rehabilitation approaches. Exploiting 
mechanism-​based biological strategies in combination 
with targeted technological interventions could augment 
neuroplasticity while directing the reorganization and 
integration of axons and relay circuits to enable clinically 
meaningful recovery after SCI.
SCI affects multiple cell types, resulting in a complex 
pathological state that is likely to require multifaceted 
combinatorial therapy to promote repair. The strategies 
discussed in this Review all enhance neuroplasticity to 
some degree, which is essential to promote recovery of 
function after SCI. Although caution should be exercised 
when translating basic research, resolving the limitations 
of preclinical studies (Box 1) should facilitate clinical pro-
gression. Further experimental fine-​tuning of preclini-
cal and clinical combinatorial therapies will be required 
to identify the most effective synergistic strategies to 
facilitate clinically meaningful recovery after SCI.
Published online 14 November 2019
Box 4 | Challenges and opportunities in spinal cord injury treatment
Neuronal activity
The mechanisms surrounding neuronal activity after spinal cord injury (SCI), including 
following neurorehabilitation and neuromodulation, are still poorly defined and should 
be a priority for future research. Pharmacological targeting of novel activity-​dependent 
mechanisms, in combination with rapidly evolving neurorehabilitative strategies, could 
further enhance functional recovery.
Cell signalling
We need to better define cell signalling mechanisms in discrete neuronal populations 
associated with specific neurological functions in the context of neuroplasticity and 
regeneration after SCI. The array of molecular targets to promote axonal regeneration 
and plasticity could be extended by single-​cell studies performed during neuronal 
development and maturation, with the aim of identifying strategies to recapitulate 
developmental pathways following axonal injury in the adult. Identifying the differences 
between nonregenerative mammalian and regenerative nonmammalian models would 
also be useful. The development of single-​cell RNA sequencing analysis, coupled with 
mouse genetics, viral vectors and viral tracing, promises to deepen our molecular 
understanding at the single-​cell level and facilitate the development of vectors that can 
modulate neuronal activity and synaptic function. In the future, this technology might 
allow pharmacological or gene-​mediated targeting of specific molecular modifiers of 
neuroplasticity in selected neuronal populations to promote functional recovery after SCI.
Novel screens
The development of large libraries of small molecules, combined with CRISPR–Cas9 
gene-​editing technology, will offer novel opportunities for large-​scale screening 
studies in cells. These screens might be followed by tests of candidate small-​molecule 
drugs in mammalian SCI models.
Computational power for collaboration
Increased access to research tools should facilitate cooperation between experts in 
neurorehabilitation, neuronal activity, and cell and molecular biology. The integration 
of computational bioinformatics approaches, using existing and expanding databases, 
including the recent creation of a shareable online data repository for SCI research165,  
is likely to enhance the potential to predict the efficacy of combinatorial treatments for 
SCI repair.
