Spinal cord regeneration screen in zebrafish
PLOS Genetics | https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pgen.1009515
April 29, 2021
13 / 21

Hexb activity assay
Hexb activity was determined as previously described [58]. Briefly, 3 dpf embryos, 20 per
clutch, 3 independent clutches, were homogenised in 100 μl of nuclease free water. Each sam-
ple was diluted 1/10 with McIlvaine citrate–phosphate buffer pH 4.5 and activity assayed with
4-methylumbelliferyl-2-acetamido- 2-deoxy-β-D-gluco-pyranoside (Sigma, Dorset, UK) in a
plate reader.
Immunohistochemistry on whole-mount larvae
All incubations were performed at room temperature unless stated otherwise. At the time
point of interest, larvae were fixed in 4% PFA-PBS containing 1% DMSO at 4˚C overnight.
After washes in PBS, larvae were washed in PBTx. After permeabilization by incubation in
PBS containing 2 mg/ml Collagenase (Sigma) for 25 min larvae were washed in PBTx. They
were then incubated in blocking buffer for 2 h and incubated with primary antibody (anti-
MPX, GeneTex GTX128379, Irvine, California, USA) diluted in blocking buffer at 4˚C over-
night. On the following day, larvae were washed 3 x in PBTx, followed by incubation with sec-
ondary antibody diluted in blocking buffer at 4˚C overnight. The next day, larvae were washed
three times in PBTx and once in PBS for 15 min each, before mounting in 70% glycerol.
For whole mount immunostaining of acetylated tubulin (Sigma T6793) and GFAP (Dako
Z0334) to visualise the axons and the glial processes, respectively, larvae were fixed in 4% PFA
for 1 h and then were dehydrated in 25% 50%, 75% MeOH in 0.1% Tween in PBS, transferred
to 100% MeOH and then stored at -20˚C overnight. The next day, head and tail were removed,
and the samples were incubated in pre-chilled 100% acetone at -20˚C for 10 min. Thereafter,
larvae were washed and digested with Proteinase K (10 μg/ml) for 15 min at room temperature
and re-fixed in 4% PFA. After washes, the larvae were incubated with 4% BSA in PBTx for 1 h.
Subsequently, the larvae were incubated over two nights with primary antibodies (anti-acety-
lated tubulin, anti-GFAP). After washes and incubation with the secondary antibody, the sam-
ples were washed in PBS for 15 min each, before mounting in glycerol.
Spinal cord injury
At 3 dpf, zebrafish larvae were anaesthetised in PBS containing 0.02% aminobenzoic-acid-
ethyl methyl-ester (MS222, Sigma), as described [3]. Larvae were transferred to an agarose-
coated petri dish. Following removal of excess water, the larvae were placed in a lateral posi-
tion, and the tip of a sharp 30.5 G syringe needle (BD Microlance) was used to inflict a stab
injury or a dorsal incision on the dorsal part of the trunk at the level of the 15th myotome, leav-
ing the notochord intact.
Compound incubation
SB431542 (Abcam, ab120163) was dissolved in DMSO to a stock concentration of 100 mM
and added to the larvae as indicated at a final concentration of 50 μM. The final concentration
of DMSO was 1% and the same concentration of DMSO was added to the controls. Larvae
were pre-treated for 2 hours before the injury and were incubated with the drug for 48 hours.
Ac-YVAD-cmk (YVAD) (Sigma, SML0429) was dissolved in DMSO to a stock concentra-
tion of 10 mM. The working concentration was 50 μM prepared by dilution from the stock
solution in fish water. Larvae were pre-treated for 2 h before the injury and were incubated
with the drug for 48 hpl as previously described [3]. Axonal and glial bridging was assessed
blinded to the experimental condition on three independent clutches of larvae.
PLOS GENETICS
Spinal cord regeneration screen in zebrafish
PLOS Genetics | https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pgen.1009515
April 29, 2021
14 / 21

Behavioural analysis
Behavioural analysis was performed as previously described [59]. Lesioned larvae were
touched caudal to the lesion site using a glass capillary. The swim distance of their escape
response was recorded for 15 s after touch and analyzed using a Noldus behaviour analysis
setup (EthoVision version 7). Data given is averaged from triplicate measures per fish. Between
repeated measures, the larvae were left to recover for 30 sec. The observer was blinded to the
treatment during the behavioural assay.
Assessment of axonal and glial phenotypes
Re-established axonal connections and glial connections (“bridges”) were scored at the time
point of interest in fixed immunolabelled samples (for axonal and glial bridges) and live trans-
genic animals (for axonal bridges). Larvae were directly visually evaluated using a fluorescent
stereomicroscope (Leica M165 FC) or confocal imaging (Zeiss LSM 710, 880). Larvae were
scored as described [3] with the observer blinded to the experimental condition. Briefly, a
larva was scored as having a bridged lesion site when continuity of the axonal labeling between
the rostral and caudal part of the spinal cord was observed. The same criterion was used for
the assessment of the glial bridges. Continuity of labeling was defined as at least one fascicle
being continuous between rostral and caudal spinal cord ends, irrespective of the fascicle
thickness. Larvae in which the lesion site was obscured by melanocytes or the notochord was
inadvertently injured were excluded from the analysis.
Quantitative RT-PCR
Reverse transcription of 500 ng RNA was performed with the iSCRIPT kit (BIORAD, Hercu-
les, USA). Standard RT-PCR was performed using 10 mM of each dNTP and each primer.
qRT-PCR was performed at 58˚C using Roche Light Cycler 96 and relative mRNA levels deter-
mined using the Roche Light Cycler 96 SW1 software. Samples were run in duplicates and
expression levels were normalized to a β-actin control. Primers were designed to span an
exon–exon junction using the Primer-BLAST (https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/tools/primer-
blast/) software.
Assessment of immune cell numbers
A volume of interest was defined centered on the lesion site from confocal images. The dimen-
sions were: width = 200 μm, height = 75 μm (above the notochord), depth = 50 μm. Images
were analysed using the Imaris (Bitplane, Belfast, UK) or the ImageJ software. The number of
cells was quantified manually in 3D view, on at least three independent clutches of larvae,
blinded to the experimental condition.
Experimental design and statistical analysis
No formal randomization method was used. All mutant analyses were performed on incrosses
of heterozygous animals without prior knowledge of genotype. For all experiments and analy-
ses the experimenter was blinded to the experimental condition. Image analysis was performed
using ImageJ. Power analysis using GPower (Faul et al. 2009), was used to calculate power
(aim > 0.8) for the experiments and determine the group sizes accordingly. Statistical power
was > 0.8 for all experiments. All quantitative data were tested for normality and analyzed
with parametric and non-parametric tests as appropriate. The statistical analysis was per-
formed using IBM SPSS Statistics 23.0. Shapiro-Wilk’s W-test was used in order to assess the
normality of the data. Kruskal-Wallis test followed by Dunn’s multiple comparisons, One-way
PLOS GENETICS
Spinal cord regeneration screen in zebrafish
PLOS Genetics | https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pgen.1009515
April 29, 2021
15 / 21

ANOVA followed by Bonferroni multiple comparisons test, two-way ANOVA, followed by
Bonferroni multiple comparisons, t-test, Mann–Whitney U test or Fischer’s exact test were
used, as indicated in the figure legends. P< 0.05, P< 0.01, P< 0.001, n.s. indicates no sig-
nificance. Error bars indicate the standard error of the mean (SEM). The figures were prepared
with Adobe Photoshop CC and Adobe Illustrator CC. Graphs were generated using GraphPad
Prism 7.
Supporting information
S1 Fig. Injecting two haCRs simultaneously effectively disrupts gene function. A-C: Direct
sequencing of mutant alleles in embryos injected with two haCRs per gene demonstrates
induction of frameshift frequencies of 72% (A; haCr #1) and 60% (B; haCR #2), when either
sCrRNA is analysed individually. This rises to 87% when frame-shift frequencies are combined
(C). D: At the protein level, dual haCR injection against hexb reduces enzyme activity by 80%
in vivo (ANOVA with Tukey post-test; p < 0.0001). Single haCRs also reduce enzyme activity
(p < 0.0001 for both). haCR#2 is more efficient than haCR#1 (p = 0.0051). Error bars represent
SEM.
(TIF)
S2 Fig. Inhibition of axon bridging is observed for some of the selected genes at 24 hpl. Sig-
nificant reductions in bridging were detected after acute injection of haCRs for sparc
(p = 0.0424), tnfa (p = 0.0068), tgfb1a (p = 0.0225) and cst7 (p = 0.0418). Fisher’s Exact test,
p<0.05.
(TIF)
S3 Fig. Mutations likely lead to non-functional protein. Deletions are shown in red and
insertions are shown in blue. All stable mutations produce frameshifts (cst7, tnfa, tgfb1a and
sparc) and premature stop codons, with the exception of the mutation in the tgfb3 gene. The
latter contains an in-frame indel in which the large quantity of inserted material contains a
nonsense mutation.
(TIF)
S4 Fig. Inhibiting Tgf signalling with SB31442 impairs axonal bridging. Experimental time-
line, lateral views of embryos and quantification are shown. Fisher’s Exact test, p<0.05. The
white arrow indicates the axonal bridge. Scale bar 50 μm.
(TIF)
S5 Fig. Glial bridging is impaired in the tgfb3 mutant. Lateral views of whole-mounted lar-
vae at 5 dpf are shown; rostral is left, dorsal is up. A: Gfap immunohistochemistry shows longi-
tudinal processes over the injury site (centred in right column) for wildtype (WT), sparc, tnfa,
and tgfb1a mutants, but not for tgfb3 mutants at 48 hours post-injury (hpf). B: Quantification
of the phenotypes shows a significant reduction in the proportion of larvae with glial bridging
only for tgfb3 mutants (Fisher’s Exact test).
(TIF)
S6 Fig. Functional recovery is not significantly impaired in the tgfb1a mutant. No change is
observed in the distance that tgfb1a mutants swim after a touch evoked stimulus (Unpaired t
test, p = 0.2590).
(TIF)

===== Kent2024_NatRevImmunol+microglia_myelin =====
Nature Reviews Immunology
nature reviews immunology
https://doi.org/10.1038/s41577-023-00907-4
Review article
 Check for updates
Microglia regulation of central 
nervous system myelin 
health and regeneration
Sarah A. Kent 
  1,2 & Veronique E. Miron 
  1,2,3,4,5 
Abstract
Microglia are resident macrophages of the central nervous system 
that have key functions in its development, homeostasis and 
response to damage and infection. Although microglia have been 
increasingly implicated in contributing to the pathology that 
underpins neurological dysfunction and disease, they also have 
crucial roles in neurological homeostasis and regeneration. This 
includes regulation of the maintenance and regeneration of myelin, 
the membrane that surrounds neuronal axons, which is required for 
axonal health and function. Myelin is damaged with normal ageing and 
in several neurodegenerative diseases, such as multiple sclerosis 
and Alzheimer disease. Given the lack of approved therapies targeting 
myelin maintenance or regeneration, it is imperative to understand the 
mechanisms by which microglia support and restore myelin health to 
identify potential therapeutic approaches. However, the mechanisms 
by which microglia regulate myelin loss or integrity are still being 
uncovered. In this Review, we discuss recent work that reveals the 
changes in white matter with ageing and neurodegenerative disease, 
how this relates to microglia dynamics during myelin damage and 
regeneration, and factors that influence the regenerative functions 
of microglia.
Sections
Introduction
Myelin health in ageing and 
disease
Microglia dynamics
Influences on microglial 
functions
Conclusions

Nature Reviews Immunology
Review article
and remyelination; ‘inactive’ lesion, a chronic lesion with robust demy-
elination, poor remyelination, mostly devoid of microglia; ‘mixed’ 
lesion, a lesion with a chronically demyelinated core and a rim of 
microglia where remyelination can occur; and ‘remyelinated’ lesion, 
a lesion with resolved inflammation and robust remyelination24. Second, 
microglia are also heavily implicated in the pathology of Alzheimer 
disease (AD)25, which is characterized by pathological accumulation 
of amyloid-β plaques and neurofibrillary tau tangles that leads to 
loss of neurons and their synapses. Although oligodendrocyte loss and 
demyelination are well-established features of MS, changes in myelin 
and oligodendrocytes are a more recent and underappreciated discov-
ery in AD (Box 1). White matter abnormalities are evident by imaging 
decades before the onset of AD, preceding neural network changes 
and neurodegeneration26, suggesting that myelin changes are an early 
pathological feature. Accordingly, oligodendrocytes undergo robust 
transcriptional changes in models of AD and early on in human AD27–30. 
However, myelin changes and/or myelin loss undoubtedly also occur in 
response to axonal pathology and loss, thereby making it challenging 
to distinguish primary from secondary myelin changes in AD.
Targeting microglia may thus represent a promising approach 
to reinstate myelin health in ageing and disease. However, the field 
is still elucidating how oligodendrocyte lineage cells respond during 
demyelination and remyelination, and the contribution of microglia 
to these processes. In this Review, we summarize recent work that 
has uncovered the dynamics of myelin pathology in ageing and dis-
ease, novel roles of microglia in demyelination and remyelination, and 
factors that influence microglial function in these contexts.
Myelin health in ageing and disease
Demyelination and remyelination
The events leading to demyelination in ageing and neurodegenerative 
disease are unclear. Although demyelination in MS is considered to 
be primarily driven by a dysregulated adaptive immune response, 
the intrinsic dysregulation of oligodendrocytes may also play a part. 
This concept is supported by abnormal transcriptomic and lipidomic 
profiles in non-lesioned or ‘normal-appearing’ white matter in MS31,32. 
In addition, infiltration of immune cells into the CNS can occur second-
ary to experimental induction of oligodendrocyte death or alteration of 
myelin composition33,34. Instability of myelin may render it vulnerable 
to initial degeneration, as structural changes in myelin occur before 
and during demyelination in mouse models35–38 and MS lesions39,40. 
Hypermyelination may precede demyelination, as seen after inhibi-
tion of autophagy41 or sustained ERBB receptor activation in mouse 
oligodendrocytes42. Supporting this postulate, spatial transcriptom-
ics of regions surrounding amyloid-β plaques in the AppNL-G-F model 
of Alzheimer disease (Box 2) revealed temporal regulation of genes 
associated with oligodendrocytes and myelination, with an initial 
increase at 3 months of age followed by a decrease at 18 months of 
age43. Furthermore, the PDGF–APPSw.Ind model of Alzheimer disease has 
thicker myelin in the hippocampus at 3 months of age44, and the 3×Tg 
mouse model of Alzheimer disease demonstrates demyelination in 
this region at 6 months of age45. Given the lipid-rich nature of myelin, 
changes in lipid profiles in ageing myelin, AD and normal-appearing 
white matter in MS suggest a potential molecular link to these myelin 
changes that may precede, or potentially initiate, demyelination46–50.
The dynamics of how demyelination is balanced by remyelina-
tion has recently been elucidated in models of ageing and neurode-
generation. By using transgenic-based tracking of newly generated 
oligodendrocytes, Wang et al.51 discovered that de novo myelination 
Introduction
Microglia are a population of resident macrophages in the central nerv-
ous system (CNS) that are increasingly shown to have crucial roles in 
its development, maintenance and response to injury1,2. These cells 
derive from erythromyeloid progenitors in the mammalian yolk sac 
that infiltrate the CNS during embryogenesis3. Microglia are long-lived 
and self-maintaining, showing limited turnover in the absence of 
pathology4–7. Following a CNS insult, microglia undergo rapid changes 
in morphology, gene and protein expression, and proliferation, leading 
to a dramatic increase in number, which is an early contributor to the 
ensuing inflammatory response. Accordingly, microglia are designed 
to be early responders to CNS pathology, with high expression of recep-
tors for signals of damage (damage-associated molecular patterns, 
DAMPs) or infection (pathogen-associated molecular patterns, PAMPs). 
As such, they have established roles in initiating or propagating CNS 
inflammation, and have been implicated in neuroinflammatory and 
neurodegenerative diseases in part on the basis of their enrichment of 
disease risk variants in genome-wide association studies (GWAS)8–10. 
However, microglia are recognized to also have beneficial roles during 
CNS development, homeostasis and after injury.
Over the past 20 years, microglia have been shown to have 
important functions in regulating the health of myelin, the lipid-rich 
membrane that surrounds neuronal axons that ensures the rapid 
propagation of electrical impulses and supports axonal metabolic 
demands11,12. In the CNS, myelin is generated by oligodendrocytes, cells 
that extend processes to form discontinuous sheaths along the axon 
in a process termed myelination. Microglia influence developmental 
myelination by phagocytosis of viable oligodendrocyte progenitor 
cells (OPCs), thereby precluding their maturation into myelinating 
oligodendrocytes13, and by engulfment of newly formed sheaths to 
refine myelination patterns in response to neuronal activity14,15. Follow-
ing loss of myelin from the axon (demyelination) such as that observed 
in ageing and neurodegenerative disease, microglia also regulate 
the regenerative process of remyelination, whereby OPCs migrate to 
areas of demyelination, proliferate, then mature into newly myelinat-
ing oligodendrocytes. This process re-establishes neuronal function, 
improves neuronal stress responses and is neuroprotective16. Microglia 
create an environment conducive to pro-remyelination OPC responses 
by secretion of regenerative factors, modification of the extracellular 
matrix and phagocytosis of inhibitory myelin debris17,18. However, 
remyelination efficiency declines with ageing and progression of neu-
rodegenerative disease, often failing altogether. This may result from 
a dysregulation of microglia with ageing and disease that influences 
their capacity to support regenerative oligodendrocyte responses.
Indeed, there are several neurological conditions in which micro-
glia have altered transcriptomes and function, concomitant with demy-
elination and failure of remyelination. In normal ageing, microglia 
adopt a dysregulated inflammatory signature, and myelin undergoes 
deleterious structural changes such that it grows in excess (termed 
hypermyelination), leading to myelin that is uncompact, thicker, 
outfolding and degenerating19–22. Microglia changes also occur in 
numerous neurological conditions in which myelin pathology occurs; 
here, we focus on two such disorders that have high prevalence world-
wide and have been extensively investigated. First, microglia changes 
occur in the autoimmune demyelinating disorder multiple sclerosis 
(MS)23, in which remyelination often fails with disease progression. MS 
manifests as demyelinated lesions that demonstrate varying levels of 
remyelination potential and are categorized into four types: ‘active’ 
lesion, an acute lesion with concurrent inflammation, demyelination 

Nature Reviews Immunology
Review article
progressively decreases with ageing. In the APP/PS1 mouse model 
of Alzheimer disease, Chen et al.52 used this same approach to reveal 
that demyelination is concomitant with remyelination, and both pro-
cesses are increased compared with wild-type controls. This surprising 
increase in remyelination is, however, insufficient to overcome the 
robust demyelination, leading to an overall myelin deficit in this AD 
model52. It remains to be determined whether similar myelin dynamics 
occur in models that harbour other features of AD pathology (such as 
tauopathy) or avoid non-physiological and potentially confounding 
amyloid precursor protein (APP) overexpression (see Box 2). Impor-
tantly, the cognitive impairment observed in both models is improved 
by enhancing oligodendrogenesis and myelination via inhibition of 
the muscarinic receptor 1 in oligodendrocytes, consistent with recent 
work indicating the importance of these processes for learning and 
memory53–55.
The cellular basis of remyelination failure following demyelination 
in ageing and disease remains incompletely understood. Following 
experimentally induced demyelination in ageing, a model in which 
the tempo of remyelination is slowed, OPCs demonstrate impaired 
maturation into remyelinating oligodendrocytes56,57. In MS, a recent 
assessment of remyelination failure at differing stages of myelin loss 
and regeneration indicated three potential underpinning causes: first, 
a reduction in myelin sheath formation by remyelinating oligoden-
drocytes in acute (active) lesions; second, pronounced oligodendro-
cyte loss in chronically demyelinated (mixed and inactive) lesions; 
or third, impaired OPC differentiation in mixed and inactive lesions58. 
Enhancement of oligodendrocyte differentiation has been the focus 
of therapies now under clinical evaluation for remyelination in MS59, 
highlighting the need to complement this strategy with those aimed at 
oligodendrocyte neuroprotection and sheath formation. OPCs gener-
ated from induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) derived from indi-
viduals with MS show similar differentiation and myelination potential 
in vitro, and in vivo after transplantation, compared with cells derived 
from healthy controls. This suggests the contribution of extrinsic 
influences to remyelination failure60,61, which complements work that 
highlights a contribution also of intrinsic factors to age-associated 
remyelination failure56,62. Intrinsic responses may be influenced by 
external factors, exemplified by the contribution of increasing CNS 
niche stiffness with ageing to dysregulated OPC responses and ensuing 
poor remyelination63. The oligodendrocyte lineage cell changes that 
underpin myelin pathology in AD are less clear. A decrease in total oli-
godendrocyte lineage (OLIG2+) cells has been documented in cortical 
grey and white matter of patients with AD64. However, it is not known 
whether this is also a feature of other CNS regions nor whether this 
finding reflects oligodendrocyte death or impaired oligodendrocyte 
maturation and turnover. The cellular responses in AD models are 
also unclear, as the increase in cells expressing the oligodendrocyte 
marker CC1 or OPC marker NG2 (refs. 64,65) may represent expression 
by astrocytes and microglia, respectively, both of which increase in 
number and are considered to contribute to pathogenesis.
A recently emerging concept is that remyelination can be mediated 
by mature oligodendrocytes that re-extend their processes, counter-
ing the long-standing view that this process is carried out solely by 
newly differentiated oligodendrocytes. This was recently suggested 
in a study whereby oligodendrocyte dynamics were assessed in MS 
post-mortem lesions, by measuring the integration of 14C into cel-
lular DNA resulting from nuclear testing; this surprisingly indicated 
that remyelinated lesions contained ‘old’ oligodendrocytes that had 
not recently undergone cell division and therefore may not have 
been derived from OPCs66. Subsequent studies using live imaging of 
mouse cortex and zebrafish spinal cord have confirmed that surviving 
mature oligodendrocytes can participate in remyelination, yet this is 
a rare process that can mistarget myelin to neuronal cell bodies67–69. 
Remyelination mediated by surviving oligodendrocytes may occur 
subsequent to a retraction or ‘dying back’ of oligodendrocyte pro-
cesses following sublethal injury, recently shown to occur in human 
primary oligodendrocytes exposed to metabolic and inflammatory 
insults70. However, the molecular mechanisms by which mature oli-
godendrocytes can remyelinate, and whether disruption of this pro-
cess contributes to remyelination failure, remain to be investigated. 
In summary, these recent studies have elucidated novel mechanisms 
underpinning demyelination, remyelination and its failure (Fig. 1).
Oligodendrocyte heterogeneity
Oligodendrocyte lineage cell responses during demyelination and 
remyelination may be mediated by diverse cell states with distinct 
functions. Heterogeneity in morphology, developmental origin, 
Box 1
Changes to white matter, 
oligodendrocytes and myelin 
health in Alzheimer disease
In comparison with the grey matter, the white matter of the 
brains of patients with Alzheimer disease (AD) has been relatively 
understudied. However, work in recent years has identified changes 
to the white matter, oligodendrocytes and myelin in AD. White 
matter hyperintensities (WMHs), considered to be reflective of 
regions of damage and visualized using MRI, have been shown to 
be predictive of AD. Brickman and colleagues191,192 observed that 
parietal lobe WMH volume in particular is predictive of both time 
to dementia onset and increased risk of AD. Another study found 
that higher WMH volumes in the parietal and occipital lobes were 
predictive of an increase in total tau levels193, suggestive of damage 
to white matter occurring before the hallmark deposition and 
aggregation of proteins in AD. Single-nucleus RNA sequencing has 
also uncovered major transcriptional changes in oligodendrocytes 
in AD, many of which occur early in disease progression and are 
associated with processes related to myelination29,30.
Robust changes to the white matter have also been observed in 
the later stages of AD when pathology load is high. Higher cortical 
hyperphosphorylated tau load has been shown to be predictive of 
WMH severity194, in addition to reduced numbers of oligodendrocyte 
lineage cells (expressing oligodendrocyte transcription factor 2; 
OLIG2) being observed in both the grey and white matter in the AD 
cortex64. Interestingly, the pattern of lesion progression in AD occurs 
in the opposite order to that of myelination, with later-myelinating 
regions of the brain being affected first in AD195–197. Whether myelin 
damage occurs before and/or after axonal pathology remains to be 
determined. Yet, it is clear that white matter changes have a key role 
in the pathophysiology of AD.

Nature Reviews Immunology
Review article
remyelination and/or myelination potential and regional distribu-
tion are already well appreciated71–74. Recent work has uncovered that 
intra-regional functional heterogeneity can exist in the oligodendro-
cyte lineage, demonstrating subpopulations of OPCs with distinct 
capacity for process remodelling, proliferation, differentiation and 
integration of neuronal activity in the zebrafish spinal cord75. Studies 
using single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) and single-nucleus 
RNA sequencing (snRNA-seq) have now revealed transcriptional 
heterogeneity of the oligodendrocyte lineage in health, ageing and 
disease27–30,71,76–86. The abundance of oligodendrocyte states differs 
with age and CNS region in both mice and humans71,77,80,81,87. There 
is considerable overlap in oligodendrocyte states between the two 
species, although an oligodendrocyte state characterized by an immune 
profile is found enriched in the spinal cord only in humans71,81, which 
is of interest as remyelination efficiency is lower in this compartment 
than in the deep white matter of the brain88.
Shifts in states of oligodendrocytes are observed in ageing and 
neurodegenerative disease, which may influence their behaviour 
and, in turn, myelin health. In MS, some oligodendrocyte states are 
more prevalent whereas others are reduced relative to controls with 
no neurological pathologies, in lesions of subcortical white matter78 
or cortex79. Strikingly, oligodendrocyte population shifts are also 
observed in normal-appearing white matter, supporting underlying 
oligodendrocyte dysfunction before significant immune cell infiltra-
tion or demyelination. An oligodendrocyte population with suggested 
immune function has been identified in MS78, in the autoimmune demy-
elination model experimental autoimmune encephalomyelitis (EAE)82 
and in aged mice89. Together with the observation that immune genes 
are in a primed chromatin state in oligodendrocytes at baseline90, 
these findings suggest a potentially active role for the oligodendrocyte 
lineage in perpetuating neuroinflammation. In line with this, OPCs 
have antigen presentation capacity in demyelinating disease and after 
exposure to the pro-inflammatory cytokine interferon-γ82,91. Several 
studies have also identified changes in oligodendrocytes and associ-
ated pathways in the brains of patients with AD29,30,83,92–97. snRNA-seq of 
AD prefrontal cortex surprisingly revealed that the most differentially 
regulated genes in all cell types are associated with myelination29, 
implicating myelin as a key component of AD pathology and the likely 
involvement of several cellular networks. An overall increased tran-
scriptional activity in oligodendrocytes in men is associated with 
increased pathology, and a decreased transcriptional response in 
oligodendrocytes in women is associated with reduced white matter 
volume and worsened cognitive decline29, overall highlighting the 
importance of a regulated level of transcription for oligodendrocyte 
health. One oligodendrocyte state is enriched in patients with AD 
versus controls, particularly in female patients29. Its appearance may 
depend on the allelic APOE4 variant of the gene APOE, as individuals 
who harbour the APOE2 and/or APOE3 variants (having lower risk of AD) 
show no distinct oligodendrocyte disease-state cluster30. Aside from 
intrinsic influences on oligodendrocyte heterogeneity, it should also 
be noted that extrinsic influences such as insult or microenvironment 
could influence shifts in oligodendrocyte heterogeneity.
Several studies that performed scRNA-seq in mouse models of 
CNS pathology have converged on the discovery of ‘disease-associated 
oligodendrocyte’ states, which are absent in the healthy CNS98,99. 
For instance, a small interferon-responsive and lymphocyte-dependent 
oligodendrocyte population was revealed by sequencing of aged white 
matter, which may be susceptible to subsequent loss, thereby con-
tributing to age-associated demyelination84; interferon-responsive 
microglia were also identified as being increased. This contrasts with 
interferon-responsive oligodendrocytes and microglia identified 
during efficient remyelination in young mice85,100, suggesting that 
acute versus chronic interferon signalling may underpin beneficial 
versus detrimental white matter responses, respectively. In addition, 
stressed oligodendrocyte populations have been identified in models 
of CNS damage85 and in peri-lesional areas in MS86. A more abundant 
oligodendrocyte population expressing high levels of mRNA encod-
ing serine protease inhibitor A3N (Serpina3n) and complement C4b 
(C4b) is commonly found in mouse models of ageing (>28 months 
old), demyelination (dietary cuprizone-induced demyelination and 
EAE), AD (5×FAD, TauPS2APP and TauP310S models) and neuroinflam-
mation (lipopolysaccharide administration)27,28,82–85,99,101,102. Pathway 
analyses of genes enriched in this population indicate the involve-
ment of senescence, dysregulated lipid metabolism and inflamma-
tory responses27,83,85,90,99,101. The dynamic changes in abundance of 
these disease-associated oligodendrocytes in EAE, first peaking during 
demyelination then subsiding during remission27,82, suggests fluid 
switches in oligodendrocyte states to and from this disease-associated 
oligodendrocyte profile. The existence of this population in the human 
CNS is, however, controversial, as SERPINA3 and C4A or C4B mRNA 
are absent in oligodendrocytes in sequencing studies of AD43,83,85, 
yet SERPINA3+ cells expressing the oligodendrocyte marker CC1 are 
detected in AD tissue by immunostaining27; this may reflect differences 
between AD and its mouse models (such as the former representing 
more advanced disease), under-representation of white matter in AD 
sequencing studies, possible extranuclear localization of transcripts 
Box 2
First-generation versus 
second-generation mouse 
models of Alzheimer disease
To date, there is no mouse model of Alzheimer disease (AD) 
that models the pathology of AD in humans in its entirety, but 
instead each model replicates aspects of the disease. These 
include amyloidopathy models that harbour mutations in the 
gene encoding amyloid-β precursor protein (APP). There have 
been many studies published using the first-generation APP/PS1 
transgenic mouse model, which contains two human transgenes 
with mutations in APP and PSEN1, resulting in overexpression of 
APP to levels approximately threefold higher than endogenously 
expressed levels198. However, this model has non-physiological 
APP overexpression, which may result in technical artefacts199. 
Newer-generation knock-in models, such as the AppNL-G-F mouse, 
which has wild-type mouse APP with a humanized amyloid-β domain 
and three mutations, circumvents this limitation by expressing 
endogenous levels of APP while still overproducing amyloid-β42
199,200. 
Further work on second-generation models, together with the use 
of models that exhibit other features of AD-associated pathology 
such as tau accumulation, will enhance our understanding of myelin 
dynamics in AD.

Nature Reviews Immunology
Review article
or expression of SERPINA3 by CC1+ human astrocytes. In support of 
differences between species, mouse oligodendrocyte signatures show 
a more robust overlap with those in MS compared with those in AD85. 
Nevertheless, the common appearance of this disease-associated 
oligodendrocyte in various pathological contexts calls for further 
investigation into its function. The commonly observed regulation of 
genes relating to lipids or cholesterol in oligodendrocyte lineage states 
in human diseases and their models30,83,85,86 suggests potential impacts 
on myelin integrity, loss and regeneration.
Microglia dynamics
Microglia transcriptional states
Microglia are known to undergo significant transcriptional changes 
during demyelination and remyelination. Sequencing of microglia 
in MS has indicated changes in transcriptional state, as shown by 
scRNA-seq of biopsy samples from acute lesions23 and more recently 
using MRI-guided snRNA-seq at the edge of chronic active (mixed) 
lesions86. In the latter study, Absinta et al.86 defined ‘microglia 
inflamed in MS’ that have partial transcriptional overlap with micro-
glia in neurodegenerative diseases. One of the identified MS microglia 
subclusters had enrichment for pathways such as lipid storage and 
response to lipoprotein particles, suggesting a potential function in 
phagocytosis and clearance of myelin debris86. However, demyelination 
and remyelination are concomitant in acute MS lesions and at the edge 
of chronic active MS lesions, thereby making it challenging to associ-
ate microglia states in MS with demyelination versus remyelination24. 
In light of this, analysis of microglia in animal models in which these 
two processes can be more easily distinguished has been helpful in 
shedding light on potential differential functions. For instance, bulk 
RNA sequencing of microglia has indicated dynamic alterations in gene 
expression relating to chemoattraction, complement, phagocytosis 
and reactive oxygen species during demyelination in EAE103,104, and 
those relating to communication with the oligodendrocyte lineage 
during remyelination following lysophosphatidylcholine (LPC)-induced 
demyelination100. In addition, scRNA-seq of microglia isolated from 
LPC-induced lesions at the onset of new oligodendrogenesis (7 days 
post-lesion) identified two ‘injury-responsive microglia’ states that 
upregulate Ccl4, which was confirmed at the protein level in the mouse 
model and in MS lesions105. Moreover, interferon-responsive micro-
glia states have been identified in remyelinating brain and spinal cord 
lesions in the LPC model100,105,106. Although the changes of these states 
during demyelination and throughout the course of remyelination are 
unknown, a complementary study tracked the dynamics of microglia 
in the cuprizone model of myelin injury and demonstrated a switch in 
predominance of states from demyelination (Spp1-expressing) to remy-
elination (Cd74-expressing)23, thereby indicating the changing nature 
of microglia heterogeneity. However, the tracking of individual micro-
glia over time during the course of demyelination or remyelination is 
required to determine whether these changes reflect a direct switch 
between states.
Microglia heterogeneity is further defined by CNS region and age. 
Building on initial work using bulk RNA sequencing to identify regional 
differences in adult mouse microglia transcriptomes107, scRNA-seq 
revealed a white matter-associated microglia state in aged mice108. 
The white matter-associated microglia had a gene signature associ-
ated with phagocytosis and lipid metabolism. Accordingly, clusters of 
microglia positive for white matter-associated microglia markers were 
found specifically in the white matter of aged mice and had engulfed 
myelin debris108. Microglia clusters of ~4–18 cells, termed nodules, 
have also been identified in normal-appearing white matter in patients 
with MS109, and although often referred to as ‘pre-lesions’, the function 
of these microglia in myelin health, damage or loss remains unclear.

Fig. 1 | Myelin dynamics: from changes in structure to demyelination to 
remyelination. In ageing, multiple sclerosis and Alzheimer disease, healthy 
myelin begins to lose its structural integrity, for example, with decompaction, 
increased thickness and presence of outfoldings, which may lead to demyelination. 
During demyelination, several oligodendrocyte states have been identified: one 
with a gene signature suggestive of immune function, one expressing Serpina3n 
and C4b, and others indicative of interferon (IFN) responsiveness and stress. 
After demyelination, differentiation of oligodendrocyte progenitor cells (OPCs) 
contributes to efficient remyelination, whereas mature surviving oligodendrocytes 
may contribute to a lesser extent, with inefficient and mistargeted remyelination. 
As disease progresses, remyelination fails, which can occur as a result of impaired 
OPC differentiation, reduced sheath formation or death of oligodendrocytes.

Nature Reviews Immunology
Review article
It should be noted that these changes in microglia heterogeneity and 
behaviour may also be influenced by external influences, particularly 
as microglia are designed to respond to molecular patterns of damage 
or infection.
The above-mentioned microglia states identified during remyeli-
nation and in ageing show some overlapping gene expression and path-
way engagement found within a microglial state commonly identified 
in mouse models of injury, termed the ‘disease-associated microglia’ 
(DAM)100,105,108,110. However, a universal definition and specific signa-
ture for DAM is lacking, resulting in different microglia states being 
classified as a single DAM state (see Box 3). This biological complexity 
is further emphasized in a study that performed scRNA-seq on live 
microglia purified from the cerebral cortex of patients with AD and 
controls, in which genes associated with mouse DAM were found in 
numerous human microglia clusters111. Furthermore, although DAM 
have been associated with phagocytosis of aggregated proteins in AD, 
whether they primarily have phagocytic functions or can adopt other 
roles in white matter requires further investigation.
Indeed, with our new understanding that microglia are hetero-
geneous during remyelination, the question now arises as to whether 
associated states have specific functions in the regenerative process. 
A recent study revealed that during the demyelination phase of the 
cuprizone model, a microglia state arises with the function of clearing 
dead cells112; whether such state-specific roles (currently defined by 
transcriptomes) exist during remyelination is unknown. One approach 
to understanding the functions of microglia states is to elucidate 
state-specific cellular interactions at the single-cell level. This has 
been achieved in the context of demyelination in EAE by a combination 
of barcoded viral tracing and scRNA-seq (termed RABID-seq), which 
revealed the importance of axon guidance molecules in regulating the 
interactions between microglia and astrocytes113. Another step towards 
elucidation of microglial function may lie in sequencing transcripts 
that are being actively translated114 or assessing protein directly. With 
respect to protein analysis, single-cell mass cytometry of progressive 
MS brain has revealed decreased abundance of homeostatic microglia 
and an increase in highly phagocytic activated microglia115. In addition, 
imaging mass cytometry of MS lesions has been used to differentiate 
between different macrophage populations116 and may in future hold 
promise for in-depth analysis of individual microglia in MS lesions at 
various stages of demyelination and remyelination. Altogether, these 
studies indicate the dynamic nature of microglia states in the context of 
demyelination and remyelination, and further investigation is required 
to align these with specific functions.
Mechanisms controlling microglia
In recent years, several mechanisms have been identified that govern 
the capacity of microglia to contribute to, and respond to, myelin injury. 
A mechanism by which microglia have recently been shown to con-
tribute to demyelination is through oxidative stress induced by reac-
tive oxygen species. Innate immune cells positive for reactive oxygen 
species were detected during demyelination in EAE117. Transcriptional 
profiling of these cells identified a cluster of microglia that express 
a core oxidative stress gene signature coupled to coagulation, lipid 
biosynthesis and glutathione pathways. This cluster was also shown to 
express genes associated with oxidative stress identified in microglia in 
MS lesions, highlighting the translational relevance of oxidative stress 
to demyelinating disease. A high-throughput compound screen to 
assess impact on primary microglia size as a marker of activation — after 
exposure to the pro-inflammatory stimulus lipopolysaccharide or the 
pro-oxidant end-product of the coagulation cascade fibrin — identified 
the glutathione-degrading compound acivicin. Acivicin was shown 
to be effective in decreasing oxidative stress and pro-inflammatory 
gene expression in microglia, concomitant with reduced demyelina-
tion and neuronal damage117. An additional context in which micro-
glia contribute to white matter pathology is after chemotherapy with 
methotrexate. In a mouse model of methotrexate therapy, chronic 
microglial activation and subsequent astrocyte activation was associ-
ated with reduced numbers of OPCs and blunted differentiation into 
oligodendrocytes, resulting in deficits in myelination and cognition — 
all reversed by depleting CNS macrophages using an inhibitor of the 
pro-survival receptor colony-stimulating factor 1 receptor (CSF1R)118. 
These studies highlight how reactive oxygen species and methotrexate 
can contribute to a damaging microglial phenotype. Conversely, recent 
work has identified peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor-δ 
(PPARδ) function in microglia as being protective against demyeli-
nation. Mice with microglia lacking PPARδ that undergo EAE exhibit 
heightened demyelination, axonal injury and tissue loss compared 
with controls119.
A newly discovered mechanism that regulates microglial function 
following demyelination is autophagy (a lysosomal degradation pro-
cess used to remove cellular contents), which is crucial for microglial 
myelin debris clearance and degradation120. Microglia-specific dele-
tion of the non-canonical autophagy regulator Atg7 and subsequent 
induction of EAE resulted in reduced recovery and impaired ability to 
clear and degrade myelin debris in vivo and ex vivo120. Interestingly, 
Box 3
Disease-associated microglia
Microglia-expressing genes associated with the disease-associated 
microglia (DAM) signature have been identified in many central 
nervous system disease contexts in both mice and humans110,201. 
However, expression of DAM-associated genes may not be limited to 
damage or disease, as these have been identified in microglia states 
during developmental myelination and remyelination100,105,108,202. DAM 
were initially described as having downregulation of homeostatic 
genes (such as Tmem119, P2ry12 and Cx3cr1), and upregulation of 
various genes including those associated with Alzheimer disease 
risk (such as Apoe, Trem2 and Ctsd). Genes such as Itgax, Clec7a 
and Cd63 have also been identified as potential DAM markers110. 
However, there has not been a consensus on what constitutes a 
DAM signature, that is, the specific genes, combination of genes 
or number of genes that must be expressed for microglia to be 
classified as DAM. This brings about the possibility of different 
microglia states being classified singularly as DAM. A similar issue 
has been raised previously whereby all microglia states have been 
proposed to be either a single state or a combination of states 
depending on the model and clustering strategy implemented203. 
To overcome these issues, it would be advantageous to have a 
universal and systematic method for clustering microglia and 
classifying the various states when analysing single-cell or single-
nucleus RNA sequencing data. This would enable clarification of 
DAM function and the contexts in which they appear.

Nature Reviews Immunology
Review article
microglia-specific knockout of Atg7 has also been shown to impact 
oligodendrocyte function, with increased oligodendrocyte matura-
tion and expression of myelin proteins, associated with increased 
susceptibility to seizures121.
During remyelination, our recent work revealed that necrop-
tosis (a form of controlled necrosis) occurs in microglia to regulate 
their functional states and support regeneration100. By blocking 
necroptosis following LPC-induced demyelination in mice using a 
small-molecule inhibitor, we discovered that necroptosis is required 
to clear pro-inflammatory microglia (iNOS+TNF+CCL2+) and encourage 
repopulation by pro-regenerative microglia (ARG1+CD206+IGF1+). 
The pro-regenerative state was found to depend on type I interferon 
signalling100, which is of interest given the interferon-responsive 
microglia states that were identified during remyelination in 
subsequent studies105,106. Data-mining of microglia transcriptomes 
in EAE revealed that necroptosis-associated genes were expressed only 
at late stages, when some remyelination is known to occur122, and not 
during peak demyelination. Overall, these data associate microglia 
necroptosis with efficient remyelination. Several studies have also 
emphasized the importance of triggering receptor expressed on 
myeloid cells 2 (TREM2) in impacting the pro-remyelination function 
of microglia. Trem2−/− mice undergoing chronic demyelination via a 
12-week cuprizone-containing diet exhibited microglial accumula-
tion of cholesteryl ester compared with wild-type control mice123, 
indicative of excess intracellular cholesterol due to an impaired ability 
to metabolize myelin components. Similarly, Gouna and colleagues124 
demonstrated the requirement of TREM2 for lipid droplet biogenesis 
in microglia following LPC-induced demyelination in mice, thus 
enabling efficient remyelination. Altogether, these studies indi-
cate the importance of specific molecular signals in regulating the 
damaging versus protective and regenerative roles of microglia in 
white matter.
Microglia–oligodendrocyte communication
Recent studies have uncovered novel molecular interactions by which 
microglia regulate oligodendrocyte lineage cell responses in various 
contexts. Our recent work revealed that although microglia are not 
required for developmental myelination to proceed, they are required 
to regulate myelin during homeostasis125. We assessed the require-
ment for microglia in myelin health using a transgenic mouse devel-
oped by Pridans, Hume and colleagues126–128 in which knockout of the 
super-enhancer Fire in the Csf1r locus (Csf1r-FireΔ/Δ) leads to complete 
absence of microglia, yet preservation of other CNS macrophages in the 
white matter, termed perivascular macrophages. This model presents 
an advantage over microglia depletion via administration of CSF1R 
small-molecule inhibitors, which target all macrophages and can cause 
confounding changes in the microenvironment129. Csf1r-FireΔ/Δ mice 
demonstrated excess myelin growth followed by spontaneous focal 
demyelination, indicating a crucial role for microglia in myelin integrity 
and maintenance. Both pathological features were recapitulated in the 
rare human disorder adult-onset leukoencephalopathy with axonal 
spheroids and pigmented glia, which is caused by heterozygous CSF1R 
mutations and in which white matter microglia are markedly depleted125. 
scRNA-seq revealed the appearance of an oligodendrocyte cluster 
in Csf1r-FireΔ/Δ mice characterized by high expression of Serpina3n 
and C4b, reminiscent of the above-mentioned disease-associated oli-
godendrocyte cluster recently identified in mouse models in which 
demyelination is a common feature27,28,82,83,85,101,102. Our work uncovered 
the requirement for microglia in suppressing the appearance of these 
cells, building on previous findings that this population is regulated 
in part by the microglia-enriched phagocytic receptor MERTK dur-
ing remyelination, but not by TREM2, in mouse models of remyelina-
tion and AD28,101. We associated this population with a dysregulated 
lipid profile via pathway analyses and lipidomics, consistent with the 
observed hypermyelination125. This hypermyelination was discovered 
to be caused by dysregulated transforming growth factor-β receptor 1 
(TGFβR1) signalling in mature oligodendrocytes, with myelin health 
rescued in Csf1r-FireΔ/Δ mice by stimulation of downstream signal-
ling and pathology mirrored in oligodendrocyte-specific conditional 
knockout of Tgfbr1 (ref. 125). Given that TGFβ1 is primarily expressed by 
homeostatic microglia in the CNS, yet microglial homeostasis is lost in 
neurodegenerative disease and with ageing130, this suggests a potential 
mechanism whereby microglia dysfunction may contribute to loss of 
myelin integrity in these contexts. Importantly, levels of TGFβ1 may 
need to be tightly controlled. Consumption of a Western diet increases 
TGFβ in the brains of mice with LPC-induced demyelination, decreas-
ing remyelination efficiency131. Therefore, these studies demonstrate 
that having too much, or too little, TGFβ signalling is detrimental to 
myelin health, highlighting the need to maintain appropriate levels 
for myelin maintenance and remyelination.
Berghoff et al.132 discovered that, during remyelination, microglial 
efflux of cholesterol pathway intermediates suppresses pro-inflammatory 
microglial responses and supports oligodendrocyte differen-
tiation. By assessing cell type-specific expression of cholesterol 
synthesis and metabolism-associated genes in the corpus callosum 
of cuprizone-treated mice during concomitant demyelination and 
remyelination, the authors surprisingly found that microglia (and/or 
macrophages) had enhanced expression of many sterol genes com-
pared with low-level expression during homeostasis. Pan-macrophage 
conditional knockout of the enzymes responsible for the first step in 
cholesterol synthesis (squalene synthase) or lipid efflux (ABCA1 and 
ABCG1) resulted in reduced remyelination. Although lipid transfer 
between cell types is not currently measurable in vivo, these results 
nonetheless suggest that lipid export from microglia could act in 
two ways: in an autocrine manner to induce a microglia state that is 
supportive of remyelination and directly on OPCs to enhance their 
differentiation into remyelinating oligodendrocytes132.
Dong et al.133 also identified a novel protective role for microglia 
in responding to demyelination via scavenging of oxidized phosphati-
dylcholines, which are end products of oxidative stress and drivers of 
neurodegeneration. Oxidized phosphatidylcholines were increased 
in lesions in MS and EAE, and they were toxic to neural cells in vitro 
and in vivo. A microglia state expressing high levels of TREM2 was 
found to neutralize oxidized phosphatidylcholine deposition, and 
induction of microglia and macrophage death or use of Trem2−/− mice 
caused enhanced oxidized phosphatidylcholine levels and worsened 
neurodegeneration, altogether revealing a neuroprotective microglia 
population133. These studies highlight important molecular mecha-
nisms by which microglia support myelin health during homeostasis, 
demyelination and remyelination (Fig. 2).
Influences on microglial functions
Ageing and senescence
In recent years, studies have revealed both intrinsic and extrinsic fac-
tors that may influence the capacity of microglia to support myelin 
health and regeneration. One important contributor to microglial 
function is ageing. Cell–cell interaction analysis of scRNA-seq of aged 
brains suggested bidirectional communication between microglia and 

Nature Reviews Immunology
Review article
mature oligodendrocyte clusters, as well as the potential influence of 
microglia on OPC differentiation via release of factors such as osteo-
pontin (OPN) and insulin-like growth factor 1 (IGF1)134. In non-human 
primates, microglia in the white matter are activated with age as 
identified by HLA-DR expression, with most exhibiting a phagocytic 
phenotype likely in response to age-associated myelin pathology135. 
Indeed, microglia phagocytose myelin fragments released from age-
ing myelin sheaths136. However, the capacity of microglia to phagocy-
tose myelin debris decreases with age, which has recently been shown 
to be underpinned by downregulation of the scavenger receptor CD36 
(ref. 137) and upregulation of the negative regulator of phagocytosis 
CD22 (ref. 138). In a mouse model of impaired microglial lysosomal func-
tion, Safaiyan and colleagues136 found that microglia exhibit features 
of cellular senescence, such as impaired phagocytosis, accumula-
tion of lipofuscin granules, shorter processes and premature increased 
expression of MHC class II molecules. Cellular senescence is a state 
defined by permanent growth arrest, distinct phenotypic alterations 
and a senescence-associated secretory phenotype (SASP) of various 
pro-inflammatory cytokines, chemokines, matrix metalloproteinases 
and growth factors139,140. It has been suggested that increased uptake of 
myelin debris during ageing may lead to microglial senescence and con-
sequent dysregulation136,141. However, these above-mentioned features 
of microglia are not exclusive to senescence and can also be observed in 
aged cells. The distinction between aged and senescent cells (see Box 4) 
is crucial to consider as they likely have different effects on the CNS 
microenvironment.
Nonetheless, senescence is one of the hallmarks of ageing142 and 
can maintain homeostasis, contribute to pathological states143,144 
and support or impede repair. Senescent cells accumulate in the aged 
brain140,145,146 and are implicated in multiple neurodegenerative diseases 
such as MS and AD86,147,148. Microglia exhibit features of senescence 
with ageing147,149,150, for instance, with dystrophic morphology char-
acterized by process fragmentation and beading151–153; dystrophic 
microglia have also been identified in neurodegenerative disease154,155. 
However, a recent study established that dystrophic microglia do 
not express key senescence markers156. Together with no universal 
biomarker of senescence, this suggests that a combination of mark-
ers increases confidence in verifying microglial senescence143,144,157,158. 
Although the specific role of senescent microglia in neurodegenera-
tion remains unclear, a recent study found that approximately 5% 
of microglia in the APP/PS1 mouse model of AD undergo replicative 
senescence, induced by telomere shortening when the cell reaches 
its proliferation threshold159. Inhibiting proliferation of all microglia 
or macrophages using a CSF1R inhibitor decreased amyloid-β plaque 
density and load159, suggesting a potential role for microglial replica-
tive senescence in AD pathology. The mechanisms by which a small

Fig. 2 | Microglia dynamics during demyelination and remyelination: 
transcriptional state and function. Microglia have been implicated 
in regulating several aspects of myelin health. This includes preventing 
hypermyelination via production of transforming growth factor-β1 (TGFβ1) 
during homeostasis. Microglia can also regulate demyelination, preventing it 
via peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor-δ (PPARδ) and contributing to 
it via reactive oxygen species (ROS). Microglia states expressing osteopontin 
(OPN) are observed during demyelination, with a switch to states characterized 
by upregulation of CD74, CC-chemokine ligand 4 (CCL4) and triggering 
receptor expressed on myeloid cells 2 (TREM2) during remyelination. This 
transition from demyelination to remyelination also involves the death of 
pro-inflammatory microglia by necroptosis, followed by repopulation with pro-
regenerative microglia. Following demyelination, microglia scavenge oxidized 
phosphatidylcholines (OxPCs), and OxPC deposition is neutralized by a TREM2+ 
microglia state. Microglia autophagy and additional microglia states, including 
white matter-associated microglia, potentially ‘microglia inflamed in multiple 
sclerosis’ and others, promote the clearance and degradation of myelin debris 
and dead cells after demyelination, conducive to subsequent remyelination. 
Remyelination is further promoted by microglial efflux of cholesterol pathway 
intermediates.

Review article
fraction of microglia has such a robust impact on amyloid-β pathology 
remain to be uncovered. Nonetheless, transgenic induction of death 
of all senescent cells (p16+) in a mouse model of tauopathy in AD pre-
vented increase in glial cell numbers, tau tangle deposition, neuronal 
degeneration and cognitive impairment160, further supporting the 
importance of senescence in AD pathology. As senescence can occur 
in other cell types after CNS injury, as recently shown for OPCs148,161,162 
and suggested for oligodendrocytes85, future work specifically target-
ing microglial senescence is needed to elucidate their contribution to 
pathology. In addition to the apparent detrimental effects, senescence 
also has beneficial roles in tissue regeneration163. Thus, it remains to be 
elucidated how microglial senescence may affect remyelination and 
its failure with ageing.
Cellular interactions
The cellular interactions between microglia and other cell types can 
regulate the response to demyelination. For instance, CNS inflam-
mation is regulated by bidirectional crosstalk between microglia and 
astrocytes, through the secretion of various inflammatory mediators164. 
In EAE, microglia-derived vascular endothelial growth factor B initiates 
astrocytic vascular endothelial growth factor receptor 1 signalling to 
exacerbate EAE, and microglial TGFα acts through astrocytic ErbB1 
to ameliorate it165. Astrocytes also have an important role in the recruit-
ment of microglia to the lesion site following demyelination, and disrup-
tion of this communication delays microglia phagocytosis of myelin 
debris, subsequently delaying remyelination166,167. In a mouse model 
of spinal cord injury, microglia work alongside astrocytes to form a 
‘microglial scar’, which confines the infiltrating peripheral immune cells 
to the lesion core, thus aiding regeneration168. Furthermore, microglia 
release IGF1 to induce astrocytic scar formation following injury, and 
depletion of microglia and macrophages via CSF1R inhibition leads 
to satellite lesions168. Microglia control multiple astrocyte functions 
induced by spinal cord injury, such as proliferation, lipid processing and 
inflammatory gene upregulation, and depletion showed that many of 
these interactions are necessary for tissue repair after injury169. These 
studies emphasize the significant influence of microglia–astrocyte 
communication in the context of injury and repair. How microglia and 
astrocytes communicate in the regulation of remyelination remains 
to be determined.
Microglia also interact with macrophages derived from peripheral 
blood monocytes at the site of CNS injury. In microglia–macrophage 
co-cultures from mouse and human cells, bone marrow-derived 
macrophages suppress microglial phagocytosis of myelin debris, 
whereas microglia encourage this process in bone marrow-derived 
macrophages170, suggesting that crosstalk between these cells may be 
important for the clearance of myelin debris following demyelination. 
In a mouse model of spinal cord injury, inhibition of microglia–
macrophage communication increased CD11b and CD86 expression 
in microglia and impaired recovery, indicating an important role for 
this cellular interaction in CNS regeneration170. However, microglia 
limit the infiltration of monocytes after spinal cord injury or during 
remyelination106,169, as microglial ablation causes an increase in these 
cells in the lesion and surrounding white matter106; this points to a 
role for microglia in restricting immune infiltration to the lesion site. 
Microglia may also interact with other CNS-resident macrophages, 
termed border-associated macrophages. A border-associated macro­
phage population associated with the vasculature, termed perivascular 
macrophages, has recently been shown to influence microglial synaptic 
engulfment in mouse models of AD171; whether microglia–perivascular 
macrophage crosstalk has crucial roles in myelin health remains to be 
investigated.
Peripheral factors
Microglial function can be affected by peripheral factors. One such 
example is of low-grade peripheral inflammation being considered to 
contribute to microglia ‘priming’, a process whereby microglia become 
more sensitive to a secondary stimulus, which can result in a heightened 
inflammatory response172. Microglia priming is a common occurrence in 
ageing and neurodegenerative diseases, including MS172. In mouse mod-
els of AD, induction of peripheral inflammation by lipopolysaccharide 
injection increases microglial proliferation, decreases microglial 
process number and complexity, increases expression of genes 
encoding ionized calcium-binding adaptor molecule 1 (IBA1; also known 
as AIF1) and CD69, reduces microglial phagocytosis of amyloid-β and 
increases plaque load173,174. Furthermore, microglial activation (based on 
increased IBA1 immunoreactivity) and neurotoxicity are worsened by 
lipopolysaccharide exposure in a mouse model of Parkinson disease175. 
These findings raise the question of whether the regenerative functions 
of microglia, and in turn remyelination, could be impaired in ageing 
owing to chronic peripheral inflammation.
Another peripheral influence to consider is the gut microbiome, 
the cumulative genomes of the microbiota that inhabit the gastroin-
testinal system176, the composition of which can be influenced by many 
Box 4
Distinguishing between ageing 
and cellular senescence
The terms ‘aged’ and ‘senescent’ are often used interchangeably. 
However, their distinction is crucial. Cellular senescence is a state of 
permanent cell cycle arrest that occurs in response to damage and 
stress204. It features distinct phenotypic alterations and a specific 
secretory profile termed the senescence-associated secretory 
phenotype (SASP) consisting of pro-inflammatory cytokines, 
chemokines, growth factors and matrix metalloproteinases139,140,158. 
Cellular senescence is one of the hallmarks of ageing142, with the 
number of senescent cells increasing in the body as we age205. 
This senescent cell accumulation is thought to occur as a result 
of immunosenescence — the gradual decline in immune function 
as an organism ages206 — whereby decreased immune function 
results in reduced capacity to clear senescent cells efficiently140. 
Aged cells may be senescent, but not all senescent cells are 
formed as a result of ageing. Although cellular senescence is 
observed during ageing, it occurs throughout the life course 
and in numerous contexts, such as during embryogenesis and 
tissue repair207. Furthermore, the lack of a universal biomarker 
of senescence points to the importance of using a combination of 
markers to increase confidence in identifying senescent cells143,144,157. 
In addition, ‘dystrophic’ microglia observed in aged and diseased 
human brains153,155 have long been equated with senescent microglia; 
however, recent work has shown that dystrophic microglia in the aged 
brain do not express senescence-associated markers, suggesting a 
distinction between microglia ageing and senescence156.

Nature Reviews Immunology
Review article
factors such as diet, exercise and sleep. Many studies have identified 
distinct differences in gut microbiota composition when compar-
ing healthy controls with individuals with neurodegenerative condi-
tions such as MS177, AD178, Parkinson disease179 and amyotrophic lateral 
sclerosis180. Using a network-based bioinformatics approach, recent 
work demonstrated a role for gut microbiota in regulating immune 
responses such as the activation of microglia, and the impact of gut 
metabolites on disease-associated immune processes is increased in MS 
relative to AD, Parkinson disease and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis181. 
The gut–brain axis regulates microglial morphology during homeosta-
sis and response to insult182. In addition, microglia–astrocyte crosstalk 
in EAE is regulated by metabolites of dietary tryptophan produced by 
gut microbiota165. The role of the gut microbiome in influencing micro-
glia during remyelination has also been investigated. In a mouse model 
of toxin-induced demyelination, depletion of intestinal microbiota 
with antibiotics delayed, although intensified, numbers of microglia 
and monocyte-derived macrophages in association with poor myelin 
debris clearance and impaired OPC differentiation183. However, OPC 
differentiation was not affected by probiotic treatment or germ-free 
conditions, and remyelination in all three conditions was minimally 
affected183. Another study showed that the gut metabolite butyrate, 
a short-chain fatty acid, reduces demyelination and improves remyeli-
nation in demyelinated organotypic brain explants independently of 
microglia presence184, suggestive of a gut metabolite that can impact 
demyelination and remyelination through mechanisms other than 
those involving microglia. Therefore, the gut microbiota influences 
both microglial function and remyelination, although the causal 
contribution of microglia in this process is subtle.
Exercise has been suggested as a pro-remyelination intervention 
for MS185, and it can influence microglial function, demyelination and 
remyelination186,187. For instance, high-intensity continuous training 
in mice delays the onset of EAE, reduces the burden of disease and 
preserves axons compared with sedentary controls188. This is associ-
ated with a reduction in microglia and/or macrophages expressing the 
pro-inflammatory marker inducible nitric oxide synthase, indicating 
an impact on inflammation, yet the functional implications of this find-
ing are not clear. With regard to remyelination, quantitative shotgun 
proteomics on toxin-induced demyelinated mouse spinal cord tissue 
showed that mice that had unlimited access to a running wheel for 
4 days exhibited an increase in proteins associated with glutathione 
antioxidant responses189, which have been shown to have important 
roles in tissue repair190. A decrease in myelin protein may be indicative 
of faster myelin debris clearance in the exercising mice189. Furthermore, 
Jensen et al.186 also demonstrated a role for exercise in improving OPC 
proliferation and remyelination in the mouse model of LPC-induced 
demyelination. These studies highlight the crucial part that exercise 
Glossary
3×Tg mouse model of 
Alzheimer disease
Mice that harbour the human APP 
transgene containing the Swedish 
mutation, a PSEN1 knock-in with 
the M146V mutation and the MAPT 
transgene containing the P301L 
mutation. Unlike some of the other 
commonly used Alzheimer disease 
mouse models, this model progressively 
develops both amyloid-β plaques and 
neurofibrillary tangles, with extracellular 
amyloid-β deposited at 6 months of 
age and hyperphosphorylated tau 
aggregates observed at 12–15 months 
of age. These mice exhibit synaptic 
dysfunction before the detection of 
plaques and tangles, and cognitive 
impairment is seen at 4 months of age.
AppNL-G-F mouse model of 
Alzheimer disease
Mice that possess a single amyloid-β 
precursor protein gene (APP) knock-in, 
containing a humanized amyloid-β 
domain with three mutations, 
namely the Swedish ‘NL’, Arctic ‘G’ 
and Iberian ‘F’ mutations. Although 
APP is expressed at wild-type levels, 
the NL, G and F mutations boost 
total amyloid-β production, encourage 
amyloid-β aggregation and increase 
the amyloid-β42:amyloid-β40 ratio, 
respectively. These mice exhibit 
amyloid-β plaque accumulation with 
deposition beginning at 2 months of 
age. They also display microgliosis, 
astrocytosis and synapse loss, in 
addition to cognitive impairment at 
6 months of age. Neurofibrillary tangles 
are absent from this model.
APP/PS1 mouse model of 
Alzheimer disease
Mice that express two human 
transgenes for APP and PSEN1 
(presenilin-1, also known as PS1), 
which contain the Swedish and L166P 
mutations, respectively. Both are under 
the control of the Thy1 promoter, and the 
mice demonstrate overexpression of 
APP with levels approximately threefold 
higher than those endogenously 
expressed. Amyloid-β deposition begins 
at 6 weeks of age, with microgliosis and 
astrocytosis, and dendritic spine loss 
is also observed in the mice. Cognitive 
impairment is seen at 7 months of age, 
and neurofibrillary tangles are absent 
from this model.
Border-associated 
macrophages
Also known as central nervous system 
(CNS)-associated macrophages, these 
macrophages reside in the border 
regions of the CNS including the 
perivascular spaces, the choroid plexus 
and the meninges.
Dietary cuprizone-induced 
demyelination
In this model, mice are fed with the 
copper chelator cuprizone, leading to 
oligodendrocyte death and subsequent 
demyelination followed by spontaneous 
remyelination, which is initiated during 
the late demyelination phase and 
continues robustly over the subsequent 
3–6 weeks after withdrawal of cuprizone.
Lysophosphatidylcholine 
(LPC)-induced demyelination
A widely used model to study 
remyelination, involving injection of LPC 
to induce focal demyelination in either the 
corpus callosum or spinal cord, which is 
typically complete by 3 days after injection 
and is followed by robust remyelination 
without ongoing demyelination over the 
subsequent 2–4 weeks.
Monocytes
Monocytes originate from 
haematopoietic stem and progenitor 
cells in the bone marrow, from which 
they emigrate to circulate in the 
blood before entering tissues and 
differentiating into either macrophages 
or monocyte-derived dendritic cells.
PDGF–APPSw.Ind mouse model 
of Alzheimer disease
Mice that express the human APP 
transgene containing the Swedish and 
Indiana (V717F) mutations, under the 
control of the PDGFB promoter. These 
mice overexpress APP, with amyloid-β 
seen at 6 weeks of age and plaques 
evident at 5–7 months of age. This 
model also demonstrates synapse loss, 
astrogliosis and microgliosis, in addition 
to cognitive deficits by 4 months of age. 
Neurofibrillary tangles are absent from 
this model.

plays in regulating microglia and oligodendrocyte function, which 
may in turn impact remyelination. In summary, microglial functions in 
regulating myelin health may be influenced by intrinsic factors such as 
ageing and senescence and extrinsic factors from cellular interactions 
and peripheral stimuli (Fig. 3).
Conclusions
Increasing evidence points to oligodendrocyte dysfunction and myelin 
pathology in ageing and neurodegenerative disease. Although the 
underpinning mechanisms are still being uncovered, the influence 
of microglia on the oligodendrocyte lineage is emerging as a crucial 
determinant of myelin health across the lifespan. Recent studies have 
identified crucial roles for microglia in regulating oligodendrocyte 
heterogeneity, myelin maintenance, demyelination and remyelina-
tion. How these functions are associated with specific microglial states 
and how they are influenced by intrinsic factors (such as ageing and 
senescence) and/or extrinsic factors (such as peripheral inflammation, 
gut microbiome and exercise) remains to be fully understood. With 
pro-remyelination therapies that target the oligodendrocyte lineage 
directly currently in clinical trials, one may consider that developing 
novel protective or regenerative strategies that target microglia may 
represent an effective complementary strategy to support myelin 
health. For instance, myelin protective strategies may involve targeting 
microglial pathways involving TGFβ, PPARδ or reactive oxygen species. 
Pro-remyelination strategies could aim to enhance microglial clear-
ance of debris, dead cells and oxidized phosphatidylcholines, promote

Fig. 3 | Mechanisms regulating microglial function during myelin regeneration: 
intrinsic versus extrinsic influences. Interactions between micro­glia and other 
cell types, ageing of microglia and peripheral influences on microglia affect, or 
are likely to affect, microglial function in the context of myelin regeneration. 
Following demyelination, microglia can promote remyelination through 
limiting the recruitment of monocytes from the periphery, while monocytes 
suppress phagocytosis of myelin debris by microglia. Astrocytes may interact 
with microglia during remyelination, by affecting their recruitment for debris 
clearance; however, the mechanisms by which microglia influence astrocytes 
remain to be elucidated. Aged microglia have reduced phagocytic capacity for 
myelin debris via downregulation of CD36 and upregulation of CD22, resulting in 
remyelination failure. Still uncertain is whether upregulation of HLA-DR imparts 
increased antigen presentation capacity, or whether increased expression 
of osteopontin (OPN) and insulin-like growth factor 1 (IGF1) is involved in 
communication with oligodendrocyte progenitor cells (OPCs). Whether aged 
microglia have a senescent profile that contributes to remyelination failure is 
unknown. Exercise has a positive impact on remyelination; whether microglia 
are involved is uncertain, but they may be involved in improved myelin debris 
clearance and increased antioxidant responses, and they show reduced 
expression of inducible nitric oxide synthase (iNOS). The gut microbiome 
also has subtle positive impacts on remyelination, and is associated with 
altered numbers of microglia, their capacity to clear debris and influence OPC 
differentiation. Lastly, low-grade peripheral inflammation is associated with 
microglial priming, which increases microglial proliferation, alters microglial 
morphology and decreases microglial phagocytic capacity; whether this 
contributes to remyelination failure with ageing is unknown.

Nature Reviews Immunology
Review article
cholesterol pathway activation or boost the turnover of microglia for 
pro-regenerative function. Given the diversity of microglia profiles 
under homeostatic and pathological conditions, an important con-
sideration in therapeutic development is whether targeting specific 
states, instead of the global microglial population, could represent 
a more efficient treatment strategy with reduced off-target effects. 
With the continued development of drug delivery strategies (such as 
nanoparticles) and tracking of microglial responses in real time (such 
as by PET scans), the field will be well placed to target and manipulate 
microglia to harness their beneficial roles in the white matter.
